steps.m: Ta, Seen, Meem (special letters at the start of this chapter)
steps.n: These are special letters that appear at the start of some Quran chapters. We read each letter by its name: ط is pronounced 'Ta', س is 'Seen', and م is 'Meem'. The small marks (ٓ) show us to make the sound longer when reading.
This is the base form of the demonstrative pronoun used to point to things. It doesn't come from a root.

steps.m: that (pointing to something far away, feminine)
steps.n: Adding كَ at the end makes it specifically for pointing to feminine things that are far away. This word is pointing to ءَايٰتُ (signs/verses) which comes right after it.

steps.m: to show, point to, or be clear

steps.n: The root letters combine with long vowels to make the word for 'sign', with a round 'ta' at the end to make it a noun
Makes the word plural by adding ات at the end
Adds ُ to show this word is the subject being pointed to by تِلْكَ (those)
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb

The verb 'he wrote' changes to mean 'a book' - the thing that gets written. Like how drawing makes a drawing, writing makes a book. It's connected to the word آيات (verses) to show the verses belong to the book.
The word 'book' becomes 'the book' by adding ال (al) at the start
Added مُ at the beginning and ِ in the middle to show this describes someone/something that actively makes things clear or distinct

steps.m: the clear (Book that makes things clear)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific and ِ at the end to show it describes الكتاب (the Book) mentioned earlier in the ayah
This is a basic word that shows hope or expectation for something to happen. It's like saying 'hopefully' or 'maybe' in English, but with more hope that it will happen.
Added كَ at the end to show this is talking directly to one person (in this case, the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ). This connects to the next word بٰخِعٌ (destroying) to say 'perhaps you are destroying...'

steps.m: to destroy or kill oneself from intense grief

steps.n: Added short vowels (fat-ha) to make it 'he did' in the past
Changed to active participle form to show who is doing the action
Changed alif to alif with bar for writing style - no meaning change

steps.m: one who is destroying himself (subject of لَعَلَّكَ)
steps.n: Added tanween (double damma) to show this is the subject of لَعَلَّكَ (perhaps you are)

steps.m: to be precious, valuable, or breathe life

steps.n: The pattern creates a noun meaning 'soul' or 'self'. In this verse, it's what's being affected by بٰخِعٌ (destroying/killing).
The fatha at the end shows this word is being acted upon in the sentence
The ك at the end shows this belongs to 'you' (when speaking to one male)
This is a basic connecting word that joins parts of a sentence. Here it connects to the word يَكُونُوا (they become/are) to show what might happen.
Added لَا (not) after أَنْ to show negation
When أَنْ and لَا come together in speech, the ن blends into the ل making it stronger (shown by the ّ mark). This is just a pronunciation change, not a meaning change.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Added يَ at start to show present tense, and changed vowels to show ongoing action. This word connects to مُؤْمِنِينَ (believers) in the ayah.
Added وا to show multiple people doing the action
Adding alif at start changes meaning from 'feel safe' to 'make safe'
Added مُ at start and changed middle vowels to make it mean 'the one who believes' (like changing 'believe' to 'believer')
Added ين to make it plural. This word is affected by يَكُونُوا (they become) in the ayah.

steps.m: if (introduces the condition 'if We will, We can send down...')
steps.n: This is a conditional particle that introduces a hypothetical scenario. In this ayah, it starts a condition about Allah's power to send down a sign from the sky that would make people humble themselves.

steps.m: to want or wish for something

steps.n: Changed root letters into past tense 'he' form by adding the vowel marks َ (fatha)
Added ن at beginning to show 'we' are doing the action
The word is shortened because it comes after 'if' (إِن). The long 'aa' sound at the end becomes a short stop.

steps.m: we will (emphasized, in إِن if clause)
steps.n: Added shadda for emphasis after إِن
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
The doubled middle letter shows this happens many times or with great force
Added نُ at start to show 'we' are doing it, and changed vowels to show it's happening now

steps.m: we send down repeatedly (in a conditional statement with إِن)
steps.n: Changed the ending because this verb comes after إِن (if/when) in the ayah
The ى at the end changes to يْ when something is attached after it. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.

steps.m: upon them (connected to نُنَزِّلْ - we send down)
steps.n: Added هِم at the end to show this is happening to 'them' (a group of men). This word connects to نُنَزِّلْ (we send down) to show what is being sent down upon them.
This is a basic preposition that shows something comes from somewhere or is part of something
The shadda (ّ) is added because this word comes after نَّشَأْ which ends with a noon sound. When two noon sounds meet, they merge together making a stronger noon sound.
The fatha (َ) at the end connects this word to the next word ٱلسَّمَآءِ (the sky), showing that they go together to mean 'from the sky'
Adds اء to make it a noun referring to something high/elevated
Adds ال to make it 'the sky' instead of just 'sky'
Added kasra at the end to show connection to مِّنَ (from). The alif is written as آ to show it's a long vowel.
The root letters combine with special spelling because of the weak letters. The hamza gets a fatha and the first ي changes to alif.
The ة shows this is one specific instance of a sign

steps.m: a sign/miracle (object of نُنَزِّلْ - we send down)
steps.n: The ً shows this is receiving the action of 'sending down' in the ayah
Connected the letters and doubled the middle letter ل to show continuous action
Added تْ to show the subject is feminine (referring to أَعْنٰقُهُمْ - their necks)

steps.m: and so it (feminine) continued (subject of خٰضِعِينَ)
steps.n: Added فَ at beginning to show this is a result of what happened before
Adding damma vowels to make it a basic noun
Changed to broken plural form by adding hamza at start and alif after second root letter
Added damma (U sound) at end to show this is the subject of sentence
The alif (ا) changed to a long alif (ٰ), but the word still means 'necks' and is the subject doing the action of 'remained'
Added هُمْ at the end to show these are their necks
Added fatha to show the vowel sound on the letter lam
Added the feminine pronoun ها which refers back to their necks (أَعْنٰقُهُمْ) in this ayah

steps.m: to lower oneself, to bow down humbly

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb

steps.m: one who bows down humbly (describing أَعْنٰقُهُمْ/their necks)
steps.n: Changed the word pattern to show who does the action. This describes how their necks will be in a state of submission.

steps.m: ones who bow down humbly (plural, describing أَعْنٰقُهُمْ/their necks)
steps.n: Added ين to show this describes multiple things (the necks). Also changed the alif to a special long alif (ٰ) which is common in Quranic writing.
Basic negative particle that makes the verb after it negative. Here it negates يَأْتِيهِم (comes to them) to mean 'does not come to them'
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
Connected the root letters together
Added يَ at the start to show 'he' doing the action in present time
Added هِم at the end to show who receives the action
Added kasra to show this is a preposition that connects words
Added shadda for emphasis because it follows a sukun in the previous word ذِكْرٍ. This is a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: reminder, mention (object of يَأْتِيهِم - comes to them)
steps.n: Creates a noun meaning the result or effect of reminding/mentioning. This word is what 'comes to them' in the ayah.

steps.m: a reminder, a mention (object of يَأْتِيهِم - comes to them)
steps.n: The tanwin (two kasras) at the end makes this indefinite, like saying 'a reminder' instead of 'the reminder'
Basic preposition meaning 'from'. The fatha (َ) at the end helps it connect smoothly to the next word ٱلرَّحْمٰنِ (the Most Merciful).
The shadda (ّ) is added because this word comes after a vowel sound at the end of the previous word (يَأْتِيهِم). This makes it easier to pronounce.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed to show someone who has lots and lots of this quality
The definite article makes it refer specifically to Allah as The Most Merciful
The long alif (ٰ) shows this is a special name of Allah

steps.m: The Most Merciful (possessed by/connected to something)
steps.n: The ِ at the end shows this word is connected to something after it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changes from 'happened' to 'newly occurring' by adding مُ at start. Like changing 'happen' to 'happening'. Describes the reminder (ذِكْرٍ) as fresh and new.
This is the base form of the exception particle. It shows that what comes after it is different or excluded from what came before.
Adding shadda emphasizes the exclusion meaning

steps.m: except (in this ayah, it excludes what comes after it from the previous statement about 'no reminder comes to them')
steps.n: The alif at the end is the normal spelling of this word meaning 'except'. It excludes what comes after it from the previous statement - like saying 'everyone EXCEPT them'.
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter). This is a common pattern in Arabic.

steps.m: they were (subject of يَأْتِيهِم - comes to them)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show multiple people (they) did the action. This word connects to يَأْتِيهِم in the ayah, showing who the action is happening to.
This is a preposition that shows moving away from or turning away from something

steps.m: away from him/it (referring to ذِكْرٍ - the reminder)
steps.n: Added هُ at the end to show what is being turned away from. Here it refers back to ذِكْرٍ (the reminder) mentioned earlier in the verse.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changes verb 'turned away' into 'someone who turns away' by adding mu- prefix
The ين ending shows this is plural (many people) and is being acted upon by the verb كَانُوا (they were). The َ at the end shows it's receiving the action.
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation. قَدْ is a particle that emphasizes certainty or completion of an action.

Added فَ at the beginning to connect this with the previous statement. This shows that one thing follows from another, like saying 'and so' or 'then' in English. Here it connects to the verb كَذَّبُوا (they denied).
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
The doubled middle letter shows intensity - it's not just simple denial but forceful rejection
Added the plural ending وا to show multiple people did the action. This word is connected to فَسَيَأْتِيهِمْ (then there will come to them) in the ayah.
Added يَ at beginning to show present tense, with hamza getting sukun and final letter getting kasra
Future tense marker سَ is added before the present tense verb to indicate future action

steps.m: then he will come (after كَذَّبُوا۟ - they denied)
steps.n: Added coordinating conjunction فَ (then/so)

steps.m: then it will come to them (news of what they mocked)
steps.n: Added هِمْ (meaning 'them') at the end to show who will receive the news
Added vowels to connect the letters and make a basic noun
Changed to broken plural form by adding أ at start and ا after ب
Changed ending to show this is the subject doing the action in the sentence. This word is what كَانُوا۟ (they were) is talking about.
This is a relative pronoun that refers back to something mentioned before. Here it connects to يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (they were mocking), showing what they were mocking at.

steps.m: to exist or be in a state

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas in a past tense verb
Added وا to show multiple people did the action. This word is the subject of يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (they mock) in this ayah.
Added kasra to show this is a preposition meaning with/by
Added هِ to show what/who is being referred to (it/him)
Added small alif (ۦ) after هِ as a pronunciation guide. This word is what they are mocking in يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (they mock).

steps.m: to mock or make fun of

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added است at the beginning to show doing the action repeatedly or seeking to mock others
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action
This turns what follows into a question, like adding a question mark in English
Adds 'and' to connect this question to what came before it

steps.m: and have they not? (connected to يَرَوْا - they see)
steps.n: Adds 'not' to make this a negative question. The whole phrase now means 'and have they not?' which connects to the next word يَرَوْا (they see) to ask 'and have they not seen?'
Basic past tense form of the verb
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
The weak letters change to make it easier to say
Adding و changes it from 'he' to 'they'
Adding ا makes it subjunctive mood (that/to/so that they see)

steps.m: they see (addressing the disbelievers in أَوَلَمْ)
steps.n: The small و above the ا (called a dagger alif) shows this is plural 'they' and refers to the disbelievers mentioned earlier
This is a basic preposition that shows direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects to ٱلْأَرْضِ (the earth) to show what they should look towards.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make them pronounceable
Adding ال makes it definite (specific)
Adding kasra shows this word comes after إِلَى (to/towards) in the ayah

steps.m: how many (referring to أَنۢبَتْنَا - We caused to grow)
steps.n: This is a question word asking about the quantity of what Allah caused to grow on the earth. It shows amazement at the abundance of Allah's creation.
Connected the root letters with fatha to create basic past tense verb
Adding أَ at the beginning makes the meaning causative - showing someone/something caused the growth to happen
Adding نَا at the end shows 'we' did the action. The sukun (ْ) is added before نَا according to Arabic verb rules.
Basic preposition meaning 'in' - this is a particle without a root
Added kasra vowel mark to show correct pronunciation

steps.m: in it (feminine, referring to الْأَرْضِ/the earth)
steps.n: Added ها at the end to show this means 'in it'. The ها refers back to 'the earth' (الْأَرْضِ) which was mentioned earlier in the verse.
Basic preposition showing where something comes from or what it's made of. Here it connects 'how many' (كَمْ) to 'kinds' (زَوْجٍ) to show what is being counted.
The doubled middle letter makes the meaning stronger and more complete
Added kasra to show this word is describing زَوْجٍ (kind/pair) that comes after it

steps.m: to pair or join things together
steps.n: The root becomes a noun meaning one who is paired/joined with another

steps.m: a kind or type (owned by كُلِّ)
steps.n: Root meaning 'pair/join' becomes a noun meaning 'type/kind' - like how we group similar things together. Owned by كُلِّ (every/all) in this ayah.

steps.m: of a kind or type (owned by كُلِّ)
steps.n: Added tanween kasra to show this word is indefinite (not specific) and is owned by the word before it.
Added vowels and ي to make an adjective showing a strong quality. This word describes زَوْجٍ (type/kind) in the ayah.
Added tanween kasra (ٍ) to show this is describing something general (not a specific noble thing) and follows the word it describes (زَوْجٍ)
This is a basic emphasis word that makes what comes after it stronger, like adding 'definitely' in English

steps.m: indeed (emphasizes فِى ذٰلِكَ لَءَايَةً that follows)
steps.n: Adding the shadda (double letter mark) and kasra makes the emphasis even stronger. This word connects to the phrase after it (فِى ذٰلِكَ لَءَايَةً) to emphasize that something is definitely true.
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows location or position
The ى is added to complete the word. In this ayah, it connects 'in' to the word ذٰلِكَ (that), showing what contains the sign/miracle being discussed.
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun used to point to something nearby
The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) to show it's pointing to something far away instead of near
Added ل with kasra as a connecting letter that helps join the parts together

steps.m: that (pointing to something far away) (refers to the signs/evidence mentioned in إِنَّ فِى)
steps.n: Added ك with fatha at the end to complete the demonstrative pronoun. In this ayah, it's pointing to the signs or evidence mentioned in the phrase before it (إِنَّ فِى)

steps.m: a sign, miracle, or clear evidence
steps.n: Adding ة makes this a feminine noun meaning 'sign'
Added tanwin (ً) to show this is indefinite (like saying 'a' instead of 'the')
Added ل (laam) at start to emphasize 'surely/indeed'
Basic negative particle that negates what comes after it. In this ayah, it negates كَانَ (was) to mean 'was not'
Adds و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before. Links the previous statement about a sign (آية) to the fact that most of them were not believers.
Added fatha marks and changed و to ا to show this happened in the past. This is a very common change in Arabic.
Added أ at beginning to make comparative form
Added هُم at the end to show possession (their) or object (them)
Changed to Form IV pattern which shows making someone else do the action
Changed to active participle form showing who does the action
Added plural ending to show multiple believers
The doubled م adds extra emphasis to show strong belief

steps.m: indeed (emphasizes that رَبَّكَ 'your Lord' is العَزِيزُ 'the Mighty')
steps.n: This is a common word that makes what comes after it stronger or more emphasized, like adding an exclamation mark in English

steps.m: and indeed (emphasizes that رَبَّكَ 'your Lord' is العَزِيزُ 'the Mighty')
steps.n: The و connects this statement to what came before it, like using 'and' in English to join two ideas
The root carries the meaning of lordship and ownership

steps.m: Lord (one who nurtures and cares completely)
steps.n: Added fatha and shadda to make this an intensive form showing complete lordship. This word specifically refers to one who nurtures, develops, and cares for others completely.
Added كَ at the end to show this means 'your Lord'. This word is the noun of إِنَّ (indeed/verily) in this ayah, which is why it has the special ending showing it's being acted upon.
This is a pronoun used to say 'he' (for masculine things). Here it connects to العَزِيزُ (the Mighty) as its subject.
The لَ at the start adds emphasis because this comes after إِنَّ (indeed), making it extra strong like saying 'most certainly' or 'without any doubt'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
This pattern makes it an intensive adjective, showing complete might

steps.m: The Mighty One (a name of Allah)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it refer to Allah specifically, and ُ shows this is one of His names being used as a title
Added basic vowels to connect the letters together and show this happened in the past

steps.m: very merciful (describing someone who shows lots of mercy)
steps.n: Added ي and changed middle vowel to ِ to show this describes someone who has this quality strongly
Added the U sound at the end to show this word is in the subject position
Added 'the' at the start to make it specific/definite
This is a word that shows when something happened in the past. Here it introduces when Allah called to Musa.
Added و at the beginning to connect this story to what came before it, like saying 'and remember when'
Added vowels (fatha) to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he called'
Added alif after first letter to show direct interaction with someone else. This word shows how Allah called out directly to Moses.
Added the small alif (ى) at the end to show the word ends with a long 'aa' sound

steps.m: to care for, nurture, develop to completion

steps.n: Adding vowel marks creates the basic past tense verb form
The verb 'he nurtured' becomes 'Lord' (a title), with the doubled middle letter showing this is someone who nurtures completely and perfectly
Your Lord - the كَ at the end means 'your'
This is the basic form of the name Moses, borrowed from Hebrew/Egyptian into Arabic
Added the standard vowel marks to show how the name is pronounced
Added the alif maksura (ىٰ) at the end, which is the standard way to write this name in Arabic
Added the small alif (ٓ) to show this is being used as a call or address, since Allah is calling out to Moses in this verse
Basic form of the particle that connects ideas or introduces new statements
The ِ (kasra) at the end shows this particle is connecting to a command that follows. Here it connects to the command ٱئْتِ meaning 'go forth'
Connected the root letters together
Changed to a command form by adding hamza at start and kasra at end - tells one person to come
Adding alif at start helps with pronunciation of the command form

steps.m: to stand together, to rise up as one

steps.n: The root meaning of standing together becomes a noun for a group that stands as one unit. This word is what the command 'come to' (ائت) is acting on.

steps.m: the people (object of ائت - come to)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ at beginning to mean 'the' and َ at end to show this is what the command 'come to' affects
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed from a verb (to wrong) to a noun meaning 'wrongdoer'
The shadda emphasizes that this is someone who makes wrongdoing a habit
The ين ending makes it plural (more than one)

steps.m: the wrongdoers (object of اِئْتِ - go to)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it refer to specific wrongdoers. The َ at the end shows this is the object of the command 'go to' (اِئْتِ)
Changes verb root into a noun meaning 'a group that stands together'
Added fatha at the end to show this word is receiving the action. This word refers to the people of Pharaoh who are being asked 'Will they not fear Allah?'

steps.m: Pharaoh (title of ancient Egyptian kings, object of قَوْمَ)
steps.n: This is a proper noun borrowed from ancient Egyptian language. It is connected to the word قَوْمَ (people of) that comes before it.
The ending َ (fatha) shows this word completes the meaning of قَوْمَ (people of), forming the phrase 'people of Pharaoh'
This is the basic question particle that starts yes/no questions
Adding لَا changes this from a question particle to an attention-getting word that emphasizes what follows. Here it's used to get Pharaoh's attention before an important statement.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form basic past tense verb
Added ا and ت to make it mean 'protect oneself'. The و and ت combined to make تّ (double letter)
Changed to present tense with يَ prefix. The final ى changes to ي with kasra before it

steps.m: they protect themselves (referring to قَوْمَ فِرْعَوْنَ - the people of Pharaoh)
steps.n: Added plural ending ونَ to show multiple people doing the action. Changed kasra to damma before ون.
Added vowel marks to make the basic past tense form. This word starts Musa's statement to his Lord.
The و changes to ا, which is a very common change in Arabic verbs with و in the middle
Doubling the ب adds emphasis to show complete/perfect lordship
This is a basic word that adds emphasis to what comes after it, like adding 'definitely' or 'truly' in English

steps.m: indeed I (subject of أَخَافُ - I fear)
steps.n: Added ي at the end to show this is about 'me/I'. The ن gets doubled (shadda) when we add ي because these sounds blend together when spoken

steps.m: indeed I (subject of أَخَافُ - I fear)
steps.n: Added the elongation mark (ٓ) to show the sound is stretched when reading
Added vowels to make past tense. The و drops out because it's a weak letter between two vowels.

steps.m: I fear (before أَن يُكَذِّبُونِ - that they will deny)
steps.n: Added أَ at beginning to show 'I' and ُ at end to show present tense. This verb connects to the next word أَن (that) to explain what is feared.

steps.m: that (connects 'I fear' to 'they will deny me')
steps.n: This is a connecting word that joins two parts of a sentence. Here it connects أَخَافُ (I fear) with يُكَذِّبُونِ (they will deny me), showing what is feared.
Added shadda to create Form II intensive meaning
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels
Added plural masculine ending to show multiple people doing the action
Added نِ at the end to show the action is being done to 'me'
Changed to present tense by adding يَ prefix and ُ ending. This describes how his chest (صَدْرِى) feels tight.

steps.m: and he becomes narrow/tight (describes صَدْرِى/my chest)
steps.n: Added conjunction وَ to connect with previous statement, showing this is part of a sequence of complaints
Added vowel marks (fatha and sukoon) to the root letters to make the word for chest

steps.m: my chest (which feels tight, connected to يَضِيقُ - becomes tight)
steps.n: Added the possessive suffix ي with kasra before it to show ownership. This chest is described as becoming tight in the verse, showing Musa's anxiety.
This is a negative particle that means 'not'. In this ayah, it negates the verb يَنطَلِقُ (moves freely/flows)
Adding و connects this negative to what came before
Added vowels to make it mean 'he became free' in the past tense
Adding اِنْ prefix shows the action happens automatically or reflexively

steps.m: it becomes free/fluent (describes لِسَانِى - my tongue)
steps.n: Changed from past tense (he became free) to present tense (it becomes free) to show how Musa's tongue will flow smoothly when speaking
Added ا after second root letter to make it a noun. Also added ِ (kasra) on first letter to show it's a noun.
Added ى at the end to show possession (my). This word is the subject of يَنطَلِقُ (becomes fluent/free-flowing) in this ayah.
Adding أَ at start turns it into a command to send
Adding sukun at end completes the command form
The فَ shows this command is a response to the previous statement about his tight chest and tongue not being loose
This is a basic preposition that shows direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects 'send' (فَأَرْسِلْ) with 'Harun' (هٰرُونَ), meaning 'send to Harun'
Added the small alif (ىٰ) which is a writing style variation that doesn't change the meaning
This is the base form of the name of Prophet Moses's brother. As a proper name, it doesn't follow regular morphological patterns.
The alif is written as a small alif (ٰ) above the ه, and a damma (ُ) is added on ر. This is just a spelling variation and doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: Aaron (the object being sent to in إِلَىٰ هٰرُونَ)
steps.n: The fatha (َ) at the end shows that Aaron is the object being sent to in the phrase 'send to Aaron' (إِلَىٰ هٰرُونَ)
This is a basic preposition that shows direction or purpose. Here it connects to the verb 'they kill' to show who might be killed
Added و to connect this phrase to what came before, showing another reason why Moses is afraid

steps.m: and they have against me (connects to ذَنۢبٌ)
steps.n: Added هُمْ (hum) meaning 'they/them' after وَلَ (wa-la) to show who has something against Moses. The word now means 'and they have' instead of just 'and to/for'
This is a basic preposition showing that something affects or impacts something else
Added يَّ at the end to show this affects 'me'. In the ayah, this shows that the sin/charge is against the speaker.

steps.m: to do wrong, commit a fault
steps.n: This is the basic concept of wrongdoing or sin
Changed from a verb meaning 'to sin' into a noun meaning 'a sin' or 'wrongdoing'. Like changing 'to run' into 'a run'
Added two damma marks (tanween) at the end to make it 'a sin' instead of 'the sin'
The only change is a small mark (ۢ) added under the letter ن. The double damma (ٌ) was already there in both words. The meaning stays the same.
This is the basic feeling of fear
Added vowels and changed و to ا because it's in the middle of the word. This makes it past tense.
Added أَ at the beginning to show 'I' and ُ at the end to show it's happening now

steps.m: so I fear (connects to يَقْتُلُونِ - they kill me)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result of what came before. This connects to the next word يَقْتُلُونِ (they kill me) to mean 'so I fear they will kill me'

steps.m: that (connects فَأَخَافُ 'so I fear' to يَقْتُلُونِ 'they will kill me')
steps.n: This word connects Musa's fear to what he's afraid of happening. It works like 'that' in English when we say 'I'm afraid that this will happen'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense form
Changes from past tense (he killed) to present tense (he kills) by adding يَ at the start
The suffix ون indicates masculine plural subject (they)
The ني (me) suffix is shortened to just ِ (i) at the end, meaning 'me' - a common way to say 'me' after verbs
Added fathas to make it past tense. This word is connected to كَلَّا (No!) which follows it in the ayah.
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb
This is a basic negative word used to strongly deny something
Adding shadda makes the meaning stronger and more emphatic

steps.m: absolutely not! (responding to قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Adding alif at the end makes it a complete particle of emphatic negation. This word is responding to what was just said (قَالَ - he said)
Connected the root letters with fathas to make the past tense verb
Changed from 'he went' to a command 'go!' by adding i- at the start and making the word shorter and sharper at the end
Added alif at the end to make it a command for two people
Added فَ at beginning to connect this command with what came before it
Changes the basic concept into a concrete noun that represents something that shows or demonstrates
Makes the word plural to show multiple signs or miracles
Shows these signs belong to us (Allah in this context)

steps.m: with our signs (connected to اذْهَبَا - go forth)
steps.n: Adds بِ (with/by) to show how the action of 'going forth' should be done
This is an emphasis word that makes statements stronger. It's like adding 'definitely' or 'surely' in English.
Added ا at the end which is a shortened version of نا meaning 'we'. This word connects to مُّسْتَمِعُونَ (listeners) in the ayah to say 'indeed we are listeners'.
Basic word meaning 'with'
Added كُم which means 'you all' to show who is being addressed. This word connects to مُّسْتَمِعُونَ (listeners) in the ayah to mean 'we are indeed listening along with you all'
Adding vowels turns the root letters into a complete past tense verb meaning 'he heard'
Added مُسْتَ at the beginning to show someone who does the action with effort or attention
Added ون to show this refers to multiple people

steps.m: indeed, we are all listening attentively (to what you say)
steps.n: Added emphasis by doubling the م to show strong commitment to listening. This word appears in a statement where Moses and Aaron are being told that Allah will be with them, listening to their conversation with Pharaoh.
Basic command form telling one person to come
Added فَ to connect this command with what came before it

steps.m: then you two come! (command for two people)
steps.n: The ا at the end shows this command is for exactly two people
This is a proper noun with no root - it's the name/title for the ruler of ancient Egypt
Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce the name correctly: فِ (fi) رْ (r) عَ (a) وْ (w) ن (n)
The َ (fatha) at the end shows that Pharaoh is being acted upon - Moses and Aaron are being told to go to him
Changed the root into a command by adding damma (u) and sukun (silent mark) - tells someone to say something
Added فَ to show this command comes after something else
Adding the ُ (damma) vowel mark helps show how to pronounce the word correctly
This is a special word that makes what comes after it stronger or more emphasized, like adding 'truly' or 'certainly' in English

steps.m: indeed we (emphasizes that we are رَسُولُ - messengers)
steps.n: Added ا at the end to show 'we' are doing the action. This connects to the next word رَسُولُ (messenger) to say 'we are messengers'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed from a verb (to deliver) to a noun (messenger) by adding و
The ُ ending shows this is the subject of إِنَّا (indeed we) in the sentence

steps.m: lord/master (one who nurtures and cares for)
steps.n: Added fatha on first letter and doubled the middle letter to show complete mastery and lordship
The kasra ending shows this word owns or possesses what comes after it (العالمين/the worlds), forming the phrase 'Lord of the worlds'

steps.m: a distinguishing mark, sign, or world
steps.n: Root meaning 'know' becomes a noun meaning 'sign/mark' by adding the vowels a-a
Adding alif after second root letter creates a noun meaning 'world'
Added masculine plural ending to show multiple worlds/realms
Added definite article and shows this word is possessed by رَبِّ (Lord of) in this ayah
This is a connecting word that joins two ideas. Here it connects to the verb أَرْسِلْ (send), showing what is being requested.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: send! (command to Pharaoh to let the Children of Israel go)
steps.n: Changed to a command form by adding hamza (أ) at the start and changing the middle vowel to kasra (i). When you want to tell someone to do something, you use this pattern.
This is a word that shows when things are together or accompanying each other
Added نا at the end to show who is being accompanied. This word connects to أَرْسِلْ (send), making the complete phrase mean 'send with us'.

steps.m: to be a son/child or descendant
steps.n: This root is about building or creating things
Adding the vowel marks makes it a complete past tense verb
These are two different words - 'banaa' means built, while 'bani' means sons/children. They just look similar but aren't related.
Added elongation mark (ٓ) after ى to show it's connected to the next word 'Israel', making it mean 'Children of Israel'

steps.m: Israel (meaning 'one who struggles with God', another name for Prophet Jacob)
steps.n: This is a proper name that comes from Hebrew (Yisra'el). It combines two Hebrew words: yisra (struggles/persists) and el (God). This name was given to Prophet Jacob after he wrestled with an angel.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this happened in the past. This word is connected to the question that follows (أَلَمْ نُرَبِّكَ - 'Did we not raise you?')
The و (waw) changes to ا (alif) because it's a hollow verb - this is a common change in Arabic verbs with و in the middle
This is a negation particle that changes present tense verbs to past tense negative meaning
Adding أَ at the beginning turns the negative statement into a question, like asking 'Did we not?' instead of just saying 'We did not'
Doubles the middle letter to show intensive nurturing/raising. The و from the root is dropped because it's a weak letter.
Added نُ at the beginning to show 'we' are doing the action in the present tense. The kasra (ِ) under the ب shows it's happening now.

steps.m: we nurture you (referring to Moses who Pharaoh raised as a child)
steps.n: Added كَ at the end to show the action is happening to 'you' (Moses). This connects to وَلِيدًا (as a child) later in the verse.
Added kasra (ِ) to show the basic preposition meaning

steps.m: among us, within us (describing where Musa was raised)
steps.n: Added نا at the end to show this means 'us'. In this ayah, Pharaoh is reminding Musa that he was raised among Pharaoh's people.

steps.m: to be born, to give birth

steps.n: Root letters w-l-d became the simple noun 'child' by adding the short vowels
Changed the middle letter to ي to make it mean 'very young child' instead of just 'child'

steps.m: as a young child (object of نُرَبِّكَ - we raised you)
steps.n: Added tanween fatha to show this is the object of the verb نُرَبِّكَ (we raised you)

steps.m: to remain, to stay, to abide

steps.n: Basic past tense form showing someone remained in the past
Added تَ to show this is addressing Musa (you) in the past tense. This is Pharaoh speaking to Musa about how Musa stayed with them.

steps.m: and you remained (connecting to what Pharaoh said before)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) to connect this statement to Pharaoh's previous question about raising Musa as a child
This is a basic preposition that shows location or being inside something

steps.m: in us (referring to Pharaoh's household where Moses was raised)
steps.n: Added نا at the end to show who is being referred to. In this ayah, Pharaoh is reminding Moses that he was raised in Pharaoh's household.

steps.m: from (connects عُمُرِكَ to what came before)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. Here it connects 'your life/age' (عُمُرِكَ) to what came before.

steps.m: from (connects عُمُرِكَ to what came before)
steps.n: Added sukun (ْ) at the end to show the word stops here
Added damma vowels to create the noun meaning 'age' or 'lifespan'
Added kasra at the end to show this word is owned by something or comes after a preposition
Added ك at the end to show this is your age/lifespan. This word follows مِنْ (from) in the ayah.
The root contains و which is a weak letter that can change
Root meaning 'passing time' becomes a noun meaning 'one year'. The و at the end changes to ة to make it a noun.
Changes the word to show multiple years by removing ة and adding ين
Adds fatha at the end to show this word is receiving the action of 'remaining/staying' (لَبِثْتَ) in the phrase 'you stayed... years'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb

steps.m: you did (referring to what Musa did)
steps.n: Changed the verb to show you (masculine) as the doer. This refers to Prophet Musa being addressed about his past action.

steps.m: and you did (connected to previous statement)
steps.n: Added و to connect this statement to what came before it in the conversation
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic past tense verb
Changed vowels and added ة to form a noun meaning 'one instance' of the action
The ة at the end became ت to connect with a pronoun coming after it
Added كَ to show possession. This word is the object of the verb فَعَلْتَ (you did) in this ayah.
This is the basic feminine demonstrative pronoun form
Adding ٱل changes it from pointing to something to connecting clauses about something feminine

steps.m: which/that (feminine, referring to فَعْلَتَكَ - your deed)
steps.n: The shadda emphasizes the connection, and the ى is an alternate spelling of ي at the end of words
Adding fatha vowels creates the basic past tense active verb form
Changed the last vowel to sukun (ْ) to prepare for adding the person marker
Added تَ at the end to show 'you' did the action
This is a personal pronoun meaning 'you' for a male person. In this ayah, Fir'awn (Pharaoh) is addressing Musa (Moses) directly.
Adding وَ at the start connects this word to what came before it. Here, Fir'awn is listing things about Musa: 'you did that deed AND you are...'
This is the basic preposition that shows where something comes from or what group it belongs to
Added fatha at the end to help connect it smoothly to the next word (ٱلْكٰفِرِينَ). This is a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to reject or cover up truth

steps.n: Added vowels to make basic past tense verb
Changed to active participle form showing who does the action
Making the word plural for male persons
Adding 'the' at beginning and case ending because it comes after the word مِنَ (from/of)
Added fathas to make it past tense. This word is connected to فَعَلْتُهَآ (I did it) in this ayah, showing what he said.
The weak letter و changes to ا because it comes between two fathas
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he' to 'I' by adding تُ and putting sukun on the last root letter

steps.m: I did it (responding to قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Added هَآ to show what was done. This word is Musa's response to a question, where he's admitting to an action he did in the past.
This is a basic time word that points to a specific moment

steps.m: then/therefore (because of what was just mentioned)
steps.n: Adding ًا changes this from just a time word to showing that one thing leads to another. In this ayah, it connects Musa's past action (doing something wrong) to his conclusion about being misguided.

steps.m: I (the speaker in فَعَلْتُهَآ - I did it)
steps.n: This is the basic pronoun meaning 'I' in Arabic. Here it's being used by Musa to refer to himself when talking about something he did in the past.

steps.m: and I (the speaker in فَعَلْتُهَآ - I did it)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to connect this statement to what came before it

steps.m: and I (the speaker in فَعَلْتُهَآ - I did it)
steps.n: Added a small mark (۠) above the final alif to show it's pronounced clearly
This is a basic preposition that shows something comes from somewhere or belongs to a group
Added fatha at the end to connect it smoothly to the next word الضَّآلِّينَ (those who go astray). Together they mean 'from those who go astray'
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the letters

steps.m: one who is lost from the right path
steps.n: Changes from 'he went astray' (verb) to 'one who is astray' (describes a person)

steps.m: those who are lost from the right path (after مِنَ)
steps.n: Makes the word plural because it's talking about multiple people. The ين ending shows it follows مِنَ (from/of).

steps.m: the ones who are lost from the right path (after مِنَ)
steps.n: Added ال (the) at the beginning and the final vowel َ because it follows مِنَ
Added vowels to make it into a past tense verb meaning 'he fled'

steps.m: so he fled (connected to خِفْتُكُمْ - I feared you)
steps.n: Added فَ at beginning to mean 'so/then' - shows this action happened because of the fear mentioned later in خِفْتُكُمْ (I feared you)

steps.m: so I fled (because of خِفْتُكُمْ - I feared you)
steps.n: Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did this action. This connects to خِفْتُكُمْ (I feared you) later in the ayah, showing both actions were done by the same person.
This is a preposition that shows something moving away from something else, like saying 'away from' in English

steps.m: from you all (who موسى fled from)
steps.n: Added كُمْ which means 'you all' when talking to a group. In this ayah, موسى is talking about fleeing from these people.
Starting with the basic negation particle that refers to past time
Adding shadda changes the meaning from negation to timing, showing one thing completely finished before another started

steps.m: when (connecting to خِفْتُكُمْ - I feared you)
steps.n: Adding alif at the end connects this word to what comes after it. Here it connects to 'I feared you' (خِفْتُكُمْ), showing this happened after the previous action.
Changed middle letter و to ا (alif) to make past tense 'he was afraid'
The middle letter drops out and the first letter changes from fatha (a) to kasra (i) sound
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did the action in the past
Added كُمْ at the end to show who was feared (you all)

steps.m: to give generously as a gift
he gave as a gift (to موسى)
steps.n: Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. This refers to Allah giving something to Moses.

steps.m: so he gave as a gift (to موسى)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this happened as a result of what came before - after Moses fled in fear, then Allah gave him something
This is a basic particle that shows direction or purpose

steps.m: to/for me (connected to فَوَهَبَ - so he granted)
steps.n: Adding ى shows this is happening to 'me'. This word connects to فَوَهَبَ (so he granted) to show what was granted to the speaker.
Added fatha and shadda to create the noun meaning one who nurtures/cares for others
Added kasra before ي to show possession. This word is referring to Allah who granted Musa wisdom.

steps.m: wisdom/judgment (the ability to make good decisions)
steps.n: Changed from a root meaning 'judge/decide' to a noun meaning the wisdom or good judgment someone has
The ًا ending makes this a direct object, showing the wisdom is something being given (what Allah granted him)

steps.m: to make or set something in a certain way

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fathas to make the past tense verb
Added و at the beginning to connect this action with what came before it
Added نِى at the end to show that 'me' is the object being affected by the action
Basic preposition showing where something starts or comes from
Added fatha at the end because it connects to the next word ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (the messengers). This shows that 'from' and 'the messengers' go together.
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic past tense verb
Added مُ prefix and changed vowels to show this is about who receives the action of sending
Added ين to make it plural and show it follows the preposition مِنَ (from/of)
Added ال to make it specific and َ at the end because it follows مِنَ
This is a demonstrative word used to point to something far away (feminine). The ك at the end shows we're pointing far away.

steps.m: and that (feminine, pointing to something far) (refers to نِعْمَةٌ)
steps.n: The و at the beginning means 'and', connecting this to what came before. This word points to نِعْمَةٌ (favor/blessing) mentioned right after it.

steps.m: to be comfortable, blessed, or favored

steps.n: Changed the action of being blessed into a specific blessing or favor
Added tanwin to make it 'a blessing' instead of 'the blessing'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense verb
Changed first vowel to damma and added shadda to make command form
Added تَ prefix to show 'you' as the doer and changed middle vowel to match
Added هَا to show the favor (نِعْمَةٌ) is what's being bestowed

steps.m: upon (preposition connected to تَمُنُّهَا - you count it)
steps.n: This is a preposition showing something being placed on or imposed upon something else. Here it connects to the verb تَمُنُّهَا (you count it) to show what is being counted against someone.

steps.m: upon me (object of تَمُنُّهَا - you count it)
steps.n: When عَلَى connects to 'me', it adds ي with a shadda to become عَلَيَّ meaning 'upon me'

steps.m: that (connects to تَمُنُّهَا - you remind me)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that introduces what is being reminded of. It works like 'that' in English when you say 'you remind me that...'

steps.m: to serve, worship, or be devoted to

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Doubling the middle letter changes the meaning from regular service to forceful subjugation
Adding تَّ at the end changes the verb to address Pharaoh directly ("you" instead of "he")
Root letters connected with vowels to make past tense verb meaning 'he built'

steps.m: son/descendant (one who is established from someone)
steps.n: Changed from 'to build' to 'son' - these words share the same root letters because a son is like something built/established from the father
Added elongation mark (ٓ) to show this is a plural form. This word is in a construct state with إِسْرٰٓءِيلَ, meaning 'children of Israel'

steps.m: Israel (the prophet Jacob's other name, peace be upon him)
steps.n: This is a proper name that comes from Hebrew, not Arabic. It combines two words: يسرا (yisra) meaning 'he struggles/persists' and ايل (el) meaning 'God'. So the name means 'one who struggles with God', referring to Prophet Jacob's spiritual struggle.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense. This word shows what Pharaoh (فِرْعَوْنُ) did.
The و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb - this is a common change in Arabic verbs with و in the middle
This is a proper noun borrowed from ancient Egyptian language meaning the ruler of Egypt
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation: kasra (ِ) under ف and sukun (ْ) on ر
Added damma (ُ) at the end to show this is the subject (doer) of the verb قَالَ (he said)
This is a question word used by Pharaoh to ask about the Lord of the Worlds
Added و at the beginning to connect this question to what came before it in Pharaoh's speech
Doubling the ب adds emphasis to show complete mastery/lordship
Added damma at the end because this word is the subject being described by العٰلَمِين (of all worlds)
Root ع-ل-م (know) adds letters to make عَالَم meaning 'world' - like how we know/see the world around us. Used with رَبُّ to mean 'Lord of the worlds'.
Makes the word plural and shows it's owned by رَبُّ (Lord)
Adds 'the' to make it specific and shows it's part of the phrase 'Lord of the worlds'
Added vowel marks to make the basic past tense form. This shows someone did this action in the past.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in Arabic words

steps.m: one who nurtures/cares for (subject of قَالَ)
steps.n: Doubling of middle letter shows intensity of the nurturing quality, making it mean 'complete caretaker/lord'
The ُ (damma) at the end shows this word is doing the action in the sentence - here it's the one doing the saying (قَالَ)
Adds اء to make it a noun referring to something high up
Changes from single sky/heaven to plural form by adding -waat ending
Makes the word definite by adding 'the'
Changes to show it belongs to رَبُّ (Lord), with a special i-sound ending
The i-sound at the end shows this word belongs to something (in this case رَبُّ/Lord)
Added sukun to show the middle letter has no vowel
Adding the definite article makes it refer to the specific earth/ground rather than any earth/ground
The kasra at the end shows this word is connected to رَبُّ (Lord) in the phrase 'Lord of the earth'

steps.m: and the earth (connected to رَبُّ/Lord of)
steps.n: Adding و at the beginning connects this word to السَّمٰوٰتِ (the heavens) mentioned before it

steps.m: what (asking about the Lord of the heavens and earth)
steps.n: This is a question word used here to ask about the meaning/nature of something. In this ayah, it's asking about who/what the Lord of the heavens and earth is.

steps.m: and what (asking about the Lord of the heavens and earth)
steps.n: Added conjunction و to connect this question to what came before. This word continues the question being asked about the Lord of the heavens and earth.

steps.m: to separate or be between things

steps.n: Added vowel marks to make the preposition 'between'. This word connects the heavens and earth in this ayah.

steps.m: between them both (referring to السَّمٰوٰتِ and الْأَرْضِ)
steps.n: Added هُمَا which means 'them both', referring to the heavens and earth mentioned in the ayah
The word is already in its final form. Adding آ would not be grammatically correct here since هُمَا already indicates duality.

steps.m: if (introduces a condition for كُنتُم مُّوقِنِينَ)
steps.n: This is a conditional particle that introduces a condition. In this ayah, it connects to the phrase after it كُنتُم مُّوقِنِينَ (you are certain), meaning 'if you are certain'

steps.m: to exist or be in a state

steps.n: Added fathas to connect the letters and make the past tense form
The middle weak letter و drops out in certain forms, and the first vowel changes to damma
Added تُم at the end to show this is talking about multiple people being addressed (you all)

steps.m: to be completely sure of something

steps.n: Added short vowels (fatha and kasra) to make it mean 'he became certain' in the past
Changed from a verb (action) to show the person doing it by adding مُ at the start
Added ين to make it plural, showing it refers to multiple people who are certain

steps.m: those who are completely certain (connected to كُنتُم - 'if you are')
steps.n: The م gets doubled (shadda) to connect smoothly with the previous word, and the ending gets a fat-ha mark because it follows 'if you are'

steps.m: he said (introducing what he tells those around him)
steps.n: Added fathas to make the basic past tense form. This word introduces what Pharaoh says to his people.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is very common in hollow verbs like this
This is a question word used to ask about people

steps.m: to whom (Fir'awn speaking to those around him)
steps.n: Added لِ at the beginning to show direction or purpose. In this ayah, Fir'awn is speaking to those around him, asking them if they're listening.
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to create a noun meaning the area or space around something
Added fatha at the end to show this word is being used as an object or adverb in the sentence

steps.m: around him (referring to those who were with Pharaoh)
steps.n: Added هُ ('him') at the end to show who/what was being surrounded

This is an attention-getting word that Firaun uses to make sure everyone is listening before he speaks. It's like saying 'Hey everyone!' or 'Listen up!' in English. In this ayah, Firaun uses it to draw attention to what he's about to say to the people around him.
Adding the basic vowels turns the root into a past tense verb meaning 'to hear'
Added است at the beginning to show doing something thoroughly for oneself
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Changed from 'he' to 'you' by replacing يَ with تَ
Added ون to show multiple people are doing the action
Added fathas to make it past tense. This word introduces what your Lord said.
The middle letter و changes to ا, which is common for hollow verbs
Root letters ر-ب-ب become رَبّ (Lord) with doubled ب showing complete/perfect nurturing and care
Changes to u-ending (damma) because it's the subject/doer of 'said'

steps.m: your Lord (subject of قَالَ - he said)

steps.n: The doubled ب (shadda) shows this is someone who nurtures and sustains completely
Changes to show this word is doing the action (adds u-sound at end)

steps.m: and the master/sustainer (connected to رَبُّكُمْ - your Lord)
steps.n: Added و at beginning to connect this word to the previous phrase 'your Lord' (رَبُّكُمْ)
Root letters أ-ب-و joined together with vowels to make the word 'father'
Changed from single 'father' to irregular plural 'fathers' - a common plural pattern in Arabic
Changed alif to long alif (آ) for proper spelling
The kasra (ِ) at the end shows this word belongs to or is connected to something
Added pronoun كُمُ to show possession by multiple people being addressed
Doubling the middle letter makes it mean 'first' more strongly
Added ين to make it plural, referring to multiple people or things that came first
Added ال to make it specific and َ at the end because it's the object of رَبُّ (Lord of) in this ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb. This word is the main verb introducing what was said in إِنَّ رَسُولَكُمُ...
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs

This particle emphasizes the statement that follows it. Here it emphasizes that 'your messenger is mad.' The shadda (ّ) makes the emphasis stronger, like saying 'truly' or 'most certainly' in English.
The و makes it mean 'someone who does this action a lot'
Added fatha at the end because this word is being acted upon by the word إِنَّ that comes before it
Added كُمُ at the end to show this messenger belongs to multiple people being spoken to. The final ُ helps with pronunciation.
This is the base form meaning 'possessor of' which evolved into the relative pronoun

These are actually two different words - ذِى means 'owner of' while ٱلَّذِى is 'the one who/which'. They look similar but have different meanings and uses. Here ٱلَّذِى connects back to 'your messenger'
The alif at the end (ى with ٓ) is added to help with pronunciation when continuing to the next word
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added alif at start to show causing someone else to do the action

steps.m: was sent (to إِلَيْكُمْ - to you all)
steps.n: Changed vowels to show this happened to someone rather than someone doing it. This messenger was sent to the people being addressed.
This is the basic preposition meaning 'to' or 'towards'
The ى changes to ي with sukun when connecting to a pronoun
Added the plural 'you' pronoun كُمْ to show who the action is directed towards

steps.m: to hide, cover, or make unseen

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters and make a basic past-tense verb
Shows someone affected by being 'hidden/covered' in their mind, which came to mean 'insane' or 'possessed'
Adds emphasis meaning 'truly/certainly'
Adds double damma to show this is describing someone
Added fathas to make it past tense. This word is connected to رَبُّ (Lord) which comes after it, showing who is speaking.
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter)

steps.m: to nurture, care for, raise up

steps.n: Added fatha and doubled the middle letter to show someone who does this action perfectly
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject doing the action of saying (قَالَ)

steps.m: to shine/rise (especially of the sun rising in the east)

steps.n: Added مَ at the beginning to show where something happens, and ِ in the middle to make it a place noun. This changes the meaning from the action of rising to the place where rising happens.

steps.m: of the east (possessed by رَبُّ - Lord)
steps.n: Added ِ to show this word belongs to or is connected to رَبُّ (Lord) in the phrase رَبُّ الْمَشْرِقِ (Lord of the east)
Makes the noun definite by adding the definite article
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
Added مَ at beginning and changed vowels to show place where something happens
Added ال to make it specific and ِ to show it belongs to رَبُّ (Lord) from earlier in the verse
Added و at beginning to mean 'and', which connects this to ٱلْمَشْرِقِ (the east) mentioned before
This is a question word that asks about things
The وَ connects this question to the previous statement about the Lord of the East and West. It's asking what is between those two directions.
Added vowel marks to form the preposition meaning 'between'
Adds dual pronoun suffix meaning 'them both'
Adds long alif at end to emphasize it's about two things
This is a conditional particle that introduces a condition. Here it connects 'if' to the following phrase 'you understand/comprehend' (تَعْقِلُونَ)
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
The middle letter و is removed and the word becomes shorter to make a command form - 'Be!'

steps.m: you all were (addressing multiple people in تَعْقِلُونَ)
steps.n: Added تُمْ to show this is talking about multiple people in the past. This connects to تَعْقِلُونَ (you understand) in the ayah, showing these are the same people being addressed.
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
Changed to present tense with تَ prefix for 'you', with vowel pattern changes

steps.m: you all use reason (addressing the disbelievers in this ayah)
steps.n: Added plural masculine ending ونَ to show multiple people are being addressed. In this ayah, it's specifically addressing the disbelievers who are being questioned about their ability to reason.

steps.m: he said (subject of لَأَجْعَلَنَّكَ - I will surely make you)
steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense. This word is connected to لَأَجْعَلَنَّكَ (I will surely make you) as part of what Pharaoh is saying.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs
Starting with the emphatic particle لَ which adds certainty to statements

steps.m: surely if (introducing what Pharaoh said to Moses)
steps.n: Added إِنْ (if) which combines with لَ to create an emphatic conditional particle. The ئ is added between them to help pronunciation. This introduces Pharaoh's threatening statement to Moses.

steps.m: to grasp or take hold of something

steps.n: Added vowels (fat-ha) to make it past tense 'he took'
Added اِ at the start and ت after it to show doing something for yourself (like adopting something as your own). The ت will change in the next step.
The ت merged with ء to make a stronger ت sound (with shadda)
The اِ changes to ٱ at the start of words
Added تَ at the end to show 'you' did this action

steps.m: to worship or be devoted to

steps.n: Connected the letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed from a verb (to worship) to a noun meaning 'one who is worshipped' by changing the first vowel to kasra (إِ)
Added a small alif (ٰ) after the second letter to show a long 'aa' sound

steps.m: a god (object of اتَّخَذْتَ - you have taken)
steps.n: Added ًا to show this is the object being acted upon by the verb اتَّخَذْتَ (you have taken)
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create a noun meaning 'other than'
Added kasra at the end because this word is connected to what comes after it
Added ى at the end to show this means 'other than me' - Pharaoh is speaking about himself here

steps.m: to make something into something else

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Adding أَ at start makes it mean 'to cause someone to do the action'
Added لَ at start to show this is a strong promise or oath
Added نَّ at the end to make the promise even stronger

steps.m: I will most certainly make you (a threat from Pharaoh to Moses)
steps.n: Added كَ at the end to show who will be made into something (you). This is part of Pharaoh's threat to Moses.
Basic preposition showing source or origin
Added fatha at the end to connect it smoothly to the next word ٱلْمَسْجُونِينَ (the imprisoned ones). This is like adding a connecting sound to make the words flow better when speaking.

steps.m: someone who is put in prison
steps.n: Added مَ at the start and ُ in the middle to show someone who receives the action of being imprisoned
Added ين at the end to show we're talking about many prisoners. This comes after مِنَ (from/among) in the ayah.

steps.m: the prisoners (that Pharaoh is threatening to make Musa one of)
steps.n: Added ال at the start to mean 'the' and َ at the end because it comes after مِنَ (from/among) in the ayah where Pharaoh is threatening Musa
Added vowel marks to show this is a past tense verb meaning 'he said'
Changed from قَوَلَ to قَالَ by replacing the weak letter و with ا
The أ at the beginning makes this a question
Added و with fatha to connect this question to the previous statement
Added ل with fatha to add emphasis to the question
Added و with sukun to complete the compound particle. In this context, it's used by Pharaoh to express skeptical surprise at Moses's claim, like saying 'Really? Even if...?'
The middle letter ي changes to hamza (ئ) because it's a hollow verb. Added kasra and sukun to show past tense form.
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did the action in the past

steps.m: I came to you (speaking to Pharaoh)
steps.n: Added كَ to show the person being addressed (Pharaoh in this context). This makes it clear who is being come to.

steps.m: a thing (object of جِئْتُكَ - I brought to you)
steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowel marks to form the basic noun. This word is what is being brought in the phrase 'I brought to you'.
Added the preposition بِ (with) at the beginning to show how something was brought
The word gets dots under it (ٍ) because it comes after بِ (bi). The spelling of ي changes to ى but the meaning stays the same - 'with a thing'
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Added مُ at start and changed middle vowel to kasra to show who/what does the action
The م has a shadda because it merged with another م before it, making the word stronger in meaning
Added tanwin kasra to show this describes the word شَىْءٍ (something) in the ayah
Added fathas to make it past tense. This word shows who is giving the command فَأْتِ (bring it) in this ayah.
Changed قَوَلَ to قَالَ by replacing the weak letter و with ا, which is very common for hollow verbs (verbs with و or ي in middle)
Changes the basic verb 'to come' into a command telling someone to come
Drops the final ي because it's a weak letter at the end of a command
Adds فَ at the beginning to show this is a response or result - Pharaoh is telling Musa to bring his proof after Musa claimed to have one
Basic preposition showing how something is done
Added pronoun هِ to show who/what is being referred to
Added small ي (ۦ) and elongation mark (ٓ) as pronunciation helpers

steps.m: if (introduces condition for كُنتَ - you were)
steps.n: This is a conditional particle that introduces a condition. In this ayah, it connects 'if you were' (كُنتَ) with what comes before it, meaning 'bring it if you are among the truthful ones.'
Added vowels (fatha) to make it past tense 'he was'
Changed from past tense 'he was' to a simple command 'be!' by dropping middle letter and changing vowels

steps.m: you were (addressing Musa in this ayah)
steps.n: Added past tense 'you' ending. This word is part of a conditional phrase 'if you were' speaking to Musa.
Basic preposition showing where something comes from or what group it belongs to
Added fatha at the end because it connects to the next word (ٱلصَّادِقِينَ). This shows it's part of the phrase 'from the truthful ones'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Converting to active participle form to describe someone who possesses the quality of truthfulness
Making the word plural to refer to multiple truthful people
Adding ال makes it definite ('the'), and the final fatha is added because it follows مِنَ (from/among) in the ayah
Added vowels to make it 'he met/encountered' in past tense (not 'threw')
Added أ at the start to show someone is throwing something. The verb is connected to عَصَاهُ (his staff) - the thing being thrown.
Added فَ at the beginning to show this action follows from something before it
Added superscript alif (ٰ) as a pronunciation guide

steps.m: to be firm/rigid like a stick

steps.n: Connected the root letters and changed the final و to ا because it's at the end of the word. Added fatha marks to show it's a noun.
Added هُ at the end to show the staff belongs to him (Musa). This staff is what was thrown in the verb أَلْقَىٰ (he threw).
This is a word that introduces something surprising or important that's about to happen. In this ayah, it introduces the miracle of the staff turning into a snake.

steps.m: and then behold! (connects to فَأَلْقَىٰ - so he threw)
steps.n: The فَ shows this is connected to what happened just before (throwing the staff). It's like saying 'so then suddenly' or 'and then look what happened!'
Basic pronoun form used to refer to feminine things or point them out. Here it refers back to Moses's staff (عَصَا) that was just mentioned.
Added kasra (ِ) under ه to show proper pronunciation
Changed spelling of ي to ى and added fatha (َ) to show proper pronunciation. This is just a spelling variation and doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to slither or move in a snake-like manner

steps.n: This pattern is commonly used for animal names in Arabic
The tanween shows this is an indefinite noun. In this ayah, it refers to the staff that transformed into a snake.

steps.m: to make clear or separate things

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Added مُ at start to make it mean 'the one who' and changed inside vowels to show it's describing something that makes things clear
Added shadda to emphasize the clarity or evidence being strong
Added tanween to show this is describing something in a general way
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to show this happened in the past. This word is connected to يَدَهُۥ (his hand) which shows what he pulled out.

steps.m: and he pulled out (subject of يَدَهُۥ)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to connect this action to what happened before it
This is a special noun meaning 'hand' - it's not actually derived from a verb root. It's one of the few two-letter nouns in Arabic.
Added fatha to show this word is being acted upon by the verb نَزَعَ (he drew out)
Added هُ to show it's his hand. This word is what Musa drew out in the story.
This is a particle that introduces something surprising or sudden. Here it introduces the miracle of Moses's hand becoming white.

steps.m: and behold! (introduces Moses's white hand after he drew it out)
steps.n: The فَ shows this happens immediately after Moses drew out his hand (وَنَزَعَ يَدَهُۥ)
This is the base form of the demonstrative pronoun
Adding ى changes 'this' to mean 'she/it' - makes it refer to a female
The fatha at the end shows this is the subject of the sentence. Here it refers to يَد (hand) which is described as بَيْضَآءُ (white)
Added basic vowel marks to connect the letters
This form makes the word feminine and is commonly used for colors when describing feminine things. In this ayah, it's describing يَد (hand) which is feminine
The ُ at the end shows this word is the subject after إِذَا (behold/suddenly). The آ is just a spelling variation of ا
Added vowels to connect the letters and show past tense
Changed pattern to show the doer of the action
Added ending to show multiple people who observe
Added ال to specify these particular observers
Added لِ to show this is for/to the observers
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense. This word is connected to لِلْمَلَإِ (to the chiefs) as he is speaking to them.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in Arabic verbs with و in the middle
Adding fatha vowels creates basic past tense verb form
Changed final hamza form for spelling rules when kasra follows
Added the definite article ال to specify a particular assembly

steps.m: to the assembly/group of nobles (who were around him)
steps.n: Added لِ at beginning to show direction/recipient and kasra at end to show connection. This assembly was the group of nobles around Pharaoh that he was addressing.
Changes the root into a noun meaning 'area around something'
Added هُ to show 'him' - this refers to the person being talked about
Added elongation marks (ۥٓ) which help with pronunciation but don't change the meaning
This is an emphasis particle that makes listeners pay attention to what comes next. In this ayah, it emphasizes the statement 'this is a knowledgeable magician'
This is the basic form used to point to something
Added ذا to make it specifically point to something nearby that you can see. The small alif (ٰ) helps with pronunciation.
Added vowels to make it 'he did magic' in the past tense
Changes the verb into a noun meaning 'one who does the action'
Adds tanween to show this is indefinite (like saying 'a magician' instead of 'the magician')
Adds لَ to emphasize that this person is definitely a magician
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the past tense verb
Added ي between second and third root letters to show intensity of knowledge
Added tanween damma to show this is describing the word لَسٰحِرٌ (magician) in the phrase 'this is indeed a knowledgeable magician'
The و changes to ي because it comes between a kasra and a fatha
Added يُ at the beginning to make it 'he wants' (present tense) instead of 'he wanted' (past tense), and ُ at the end shows it's happening now
This is a connecting word that introduces what someone wants to happen. Here it connects 'he wants' (يُرِيدُ) with what is wanted.
Added fatha mark to help with pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Added يُ prefix for present tense and changed internal vowels to make it causative (making someone do something)

steps.m: he drives you all out (subject of يُرِيدُ - he wants)
steps.n: Added كُم suffix to show who is being driven out (you all). This verb explains what Pharaoh wants (يُرِيدُ) to do.
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from

Added shadda on م and sukun at the end. The shadda appears because this word comes after a silent letter in the previous word. This word connects to أَرْضِكُم (your land) to show where they want to remove them from.
Basic noun pattern for 'earth'
Adding kasra to show this word is being possessed by something

steps.m: your (plural) land (object of يُخْرِجَكُم - expel you)
steps.n: Added كُم to show the land belongs to multiple people. This is the land they're being expelled from in the phrase 'expel you from your land'
Changed the root into a noun by adding kasra on first letter and sukun on middle letter
Added ب with kasra at beginning to show 'with/by means of'
Added kasra at end because it's followed by a possessive pronoun

steps.m: with his magic (referring to Musa's magic in their accusation)
steps.n: Added هِ at end to show possession, with optional ۦ to help pronunciation
Starting with the question word ما which asks 'what'
Adding ذا makes the question word stronger, like saying 'what thing' instead of just 'what'

steps.m: so what? (in response to يُرِيدُ - he wants)
steps.n: Adding فَ at the beginning shows this question is a response to what came before. Here it's asking what should be done about the fact that 'he wants to drive you out of your land with his magic'
Added vowel marks to make it 'he ordered' in the past tense
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Changed from 'he' to 'you' by replacing يَ with تَ at the beginning
Added ونَ to show multiple people are being addressed

steps.m: to speak/say (especially with words or speech)

steps.n: Adding basic past tense vowel pattern
Changed middle letter و to ا and added وا at the end to make it 'they said' instead of 'he said'
Added elongation mark (ـٓ) and small high rounded zero (۟) as pronunciation helpers. This word is the subject of the following command أَرْجِهْ (postpone/delay him).
Added vowel marks (fatha) between the root letters to make 'he' form of past tense verb meaning 'delayed'
Changed from 'he delayed' to 'postpone!' by making it a command. The أ at start and vowel changes show it's a command. The last weak letter is dropped.
Added ه with sukun to show who is being postponed. In this ayah, it refers to Moses, as the Pharaoh's advisors are telling him to delay dealing with Moses and his brother.
The root letters أ-خ-و combine to make the simple word 'brother'. The و at the end disappears when used alone.
Added ا at the end because it's the object (the one being postponed/delayed)
Added و at beginning to connect this word to what came before it

steps.m: and his brother (acted upon by أَرْجِهْ)
steps.n: Added هُ at the end to show this is 'his brother'

steps.m: to send forth or raise up

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Changed from 'he sent' to 'you send!' by adding alif at start and putting sukoon at end - makes it a command
Changed spelling of alif to match standard Quranic writing style
Added و at beginning to connect this command with the previous one in the verse
Basic preposition showing location or position
Added ى at the end to complete the preposition. This word connects to المَدَآئِنِ (the cities) to show where the action happens.
Changes the word to mean a place where people live
Makes the word feminine and specific to mean 'city'
Changes from singular 'city' to plural 'cities' by replacing the middle letter ي with ائ
Adds kasra because it comes after the word في (in) in the ayah
Adds ال to make it specific cities being referred to

steps.m: to gather or collect things/people together

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed the word to show who does the gathering instead of the action itself
Added ين to show multiple gatherers. This word is what is being sent in the phrase 'send gatherers'
Connected the root letters with basic vowel pattern
Added ي at the start to make it mean 'he' in present tense
The ي at the end changes to و to show multiple people (they) instead of one person (he). Like changing 'comes' to 'come'.
The suffix ك indicates the object 'you' (singular), showing who is being come to
Doubling the middle letter makes it stronger - changes from 'complete' to 'all/every'
Added kasra ending because this word is possessed by سَحَّارٍ (magician) in the phrase 'every magician'
Added the preposition بِ meaning 'with' to show how the magicians will come
Root letters connected with fathas (a-vowels) to make the basic noun for magic
This form shows someone who does the action as their profession or characteristic

steps.m: a magician (in relation to كُلِّ - every)
steps.n: The tanween kasra (ٍ) shows this is indefinite (any magician) and follows the word كُلِّ (every) in the ayah
Adds ي between second and third root letters with vowels to create an intensive form showing complete/perfect knowledge
Adds the genitive case ending because it follows كُلِّ (every) in كُلِّ سَحَّارٍ عَلِيمٍ (every knowledgeable magician)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he collected' to 'was collected' by changing the vowels to show something was done to it

steps.m: so was gathered (referring to السَّحَرَةُ - the magicians)
steps.n: The فَ shows this gathering happened as a result of something before it. The subject is السَّحَرَةُ (the magicians) who were gathered.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Adding ة to the end makes this word mean 'magicians' as a group
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the س with shadda
Added damma to show this is the subject being gathered in the phrase 'the magicians were gathered'

steps.m: to set or determine a specific time

steps.n: Root letters connected with vowels to make the noun meaning 'time'
Added مِ at start and ا after second letter to show this is about when/where something happens

steps.m: for an appointed time (connected to يَوْمٍ مَّعْلُومٍ - a known day)
steps.n: Added لِ to show purpose/time and ِ at end to connect with the next word يَوْمٍ (day)
Basic noun form with fatha-sukoon pattern that turns the concept of time passing into the concrete noun 'day'
Added kasra tanween to show this word is indefinite (a day) and is possessed by the word ميقات (appointment), like saying 'appointment's day' in English
Added short 'a' vowels (fatha) to make the past tense verb 'he knew'
Changes the word to show something that is known rather than someone who knows
The doubled م adds emphasis to show something is very well known
The word got a special ending mark to show it describes 'day' (يَوْمٍ), meaning a specific known time
Changed the vowels to show that the action happened to someone/something rather than someone doing it

steps.m: and it was said (to لِلنَّاسِ/the people)
steps.n: Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this statement to what came before it. This word introduces what was said to the people (لِلنَّاسِ).
