Changed the root 'to be social' into the word 'people' by adding an alif (ا) between first and second letters
Added ال to make it specific ('the people'). The ن gets doubled (shadda) when ال is added because they blend together
Added لِ to show the word is being directed to or for the people. The اَل changes to لِل when combined
Added kasra at the end because it's the object of the word قِيلَ (it was said) in the ayah. The preposition لِ (to/for) comes before it.
This is a question word that turns a statement into a yes/no question. In this ayah, it's asking the people if they are gathered.
This is the basic form of the pronoun meaning 'you'
Added ت to complete the basic pronoun form

steps.m: you all (when speaking to a group)
steps.n: Added م at the end to show we're talking to multiple people
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Added مُ prefix and changed vowels to show who does the action
Added ت after first letter to change from 'doer of action' to 'receiver of action'
Added ون ending to show multiple people
Doubles the م to add emphasis/intensity to the meaning
This is a common Arabic particle that expresses hope or expectation

steps.m: perhaps we (expressing hope that we will follow السَّحَرَةَ)
steps.n: Added نا at the end to show 'we' are doing the action. This connects to the next word نَتَّبِعُ (we follow) in the ayah.
Adding basic vowelization to create Form I past tense
Adding a double letter (shadda) makes the action stronger or repeated

steps.m: we follow intensely (in response to لَعَلَّنَا/so that we may)
steps.n: Changed to present tense with 'we' as the subject. This shows what they hope to do in response to لَعَلَّنَا (so that we may)
Added vowels (fat-ha) to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he did magic'
Adding ة to the end makes this a plural noun meaning 'magicians'
Added 'ال' (the) at the beginning to make it 'the magicians' (plural)

steps.m: the magicians (as the object of نَتَّبِعُ - we follow)
steps.n: Added َ at the end to show this is the object being followed in the phrase 'we follow the magicians'

steps.m: if (introducing the condition 'if they are the victors')
steps.n: This is a conditional particle that introduces a condition. In this ayah, it connects 'we might follow the magicians' with the condition 'if they are the victors'

steps.m: to be in a state or condition

steps.n: Added short vowels (fat-ha) to make it mean 'he was' in the past
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a special type of verb with a weak middle letter
Added وا at the end to show that many people (the magicians) did the action, not just one person
This is a pronoun that emphasizes who is being talked about. Here it connects to the word ٱلْغٰلِبِينَ (the victorious ones) to specify exactly who will be victorious.
Added alif after first letter and kasra after second letter to show who does the action
Added ين to show multiple people who overcome (this word describes هُمُ 'they' in the ayah)
Added 'the' (ال) at the start to make it specific, and a fatha (َ) at the end because it follows كَانُوا (were)
Added فَ at the beginning to show this happens right after something else. This word connects to جَآءَ (came) in the ayah, showing when the magicians came.
Added vowel marks (fatha) to make it mean 'he came' in the past tense
Changed to hollow verb pattern by adding alif after first letter
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Adding ة to the end makes this a plural noun meaning 'magicians'
Added ال to specify these particular magicians. The ل combines with س to make سّ (with shadda)
Added ُ to show these magicians are the ones doing the action (saying) in this verse
Added vowels to make a past tense verb
Changed the middle letter و to ا and added وا at the end to show many people did the action. They are speaking to Pharaoh (فِرْعَوْنَ).
This is the base form of the proper name 'Pharaoh' with vowel marks added for pronunciation. The name comes from ancient Egyptian 'per-aa' meaning 'great house'.
Added fatha at the end because Pharaoh is the object (someone is doing something to/with Pharaoh)

steps.m: to Pharaoh (as the indirect object of قَالُوا - they said)
steps.n: Added لِ at the beginning to show the magicians are speaking to Pharaoh. This connects to the verb قَالُوا (they said) in the ayah.
This is the basic emphatic particle that adds certainty to a statement

steps.m: is it indeed (that we will have a reward?)
steps.n: Adding the أَ at the beginning changes this from a statement to a question. The ئِ is added between أَ and إِنَّ to help pronunciation when combining these particles.

Added نَا at the end to show this is 'for us'. This word connects to أَجْرًا (reward) in the ayah, showing who the reward would be for.

steps.m: surely a reward (acted on by إِنَّ)
steps.n: Added the accusative case ending because this word is affected by إِنَّ in the sentence. إِنَّ always puts the noun after it in accusative case.
This is a conditional particle that introduces a condition. In this ayah, it connects to the verb كُنَّا (we were) to express 'if we were'
The middle weak letter و drops out, and the vowels change to match the pattern of hollow verbs (verbs with و or ي in the middle)

steps.m: we were (the subject of قَالُوا۟ - they said)
steps.n: Added نَّا at the end to show 'we' did the action in the past. This word is part of what was said, coming after قَالُوا۟ (they said).
This is the basic form of the personal pronoun meaning 'we'
Added the ُ (damma) at the end to show this is the subject of the verb كُنَّا (we were) in this ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added alif after first root letter and kasra under second letter to show someone who does the action
Makes it plural to show multiple people who overcome. This word relates to كُنَّا (we were) in the verse.

steps.m: the ones who overcome (related to كُنَّا)
steps.n: Adds definiteness to the plural form, making it refer to specific victorious ones rather than victorious ones in general
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense. This word introduces what was said, which follows in the next part of the ayah.
The و changes to ا, which is a very common change in Arabic words with و in the middle
This is a basic word used to give a strong positive answer. In this ayah, it's answering what someone said (قَالَ).
This is a special word that adds emphasis to statements. It's like adding 'definitely' or 'certainly' in English

steps.m: then/in that case (responding to نَعَمْ - yes)
steps.n: Adding tanween alif (ًا) changes this from a simple 'then' to mean 'in that case' or 'therefore', showing the result of what was just said. Here it follows someone saying 'yes' (نَعَمْ) and shows what will happen as a result.
This is the basic preposition showing origin or category

Added emphatic لَّ with shadda to show certainty. This word connects to المُقَرَّبِينَ (those brought near) to mean 'surely from those brought near'
Added vowels to make basic past tense verb
Adding shadda makes it mean 'to make something close' instead of just 'being close'
Adding مُ at start and changing vowels makes this describe someone who receives the action of being brought close

steps.m: the ones who are brought close (after مِنَ)

Added vowels to make it past tense. This word introduces what Musa said next.
Changed قَوَلَ to قَالَ by replacing the weak letter و with ا, which is very common with hollow verbs
This is the basic preposition that shows direction or purpose

steps.m: to/for them (the ones Musa is speaking to)

Changed to make it mean 'to make something be thrown' - Form IV adds 'أ' at start to show causing an action

Added kasra at the end because this word is possessed by Pharaoh (فِرْعَوْنَ) in the phrase

steps.m: Pharaoh (a title for ancient Egyptian kings)

This is a proper noun borrowed from ancient Egyptian language

This is a broken plural form for city, meaning more than two cities

Added ون ending to show this describes multiple people

The 'ا' at the end means 'we'

steps.n: Changed from the root letters that mean 'to care for' into the simple word for 'father'
Added 'my' by putting ي at the end and kasra before it
Added لِ at beginning to mean 'to/for'
Added هِ at the end to show possession ('his'). This word is part of what was said in قَالَ (he said).

steps.m: to stand upright, to rise up

steps.n: Changes the verb meaning into a noun for a group of people who stand together (like a nation standing as one)
Adds an 'i' sound at the end to show these people belong to someone or something
Added هِ which means 'his', referring to Ibrahim (Abraham)

steps.m: and his people (who Ibrahim spoke to)
steps.n: Added وَ meaning 'and' to connect this to the previous word (أَبِيهِ - his father). This shows Ibrahim was speaking to both his father and his people.
Basic question word used to ask 'what?' about things. In this ayah, it's asking what the people worship, connecting to the verb تَعْبُدُونَ (you worship) that comes after it.
Added vowel marks to make it 'he worshipped' in the past tense
Changed from past to present tense and from 'he' to 'you' by adding تَ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: you (all) worship (addressing Ibrahim's father and people)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people are being addressed - specifically referring to Ibrahim's father and people who are mentioned in the ayah
Added fathas to connect the letters and make it past tense
Changed from قَوَلَ by replacing weak letter و with ا

steps.m: they said (subject of نَعْبُدُ - we worship)
steps.n: Added وا to show multiple people did the action. This word connects to the next word نَعْبُدُ (we worship) as they are speaking what comes next.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, sukun on middle letter, and damma at end

steps.m: we worship (what they say in response to قَالُوا)
steps.n: Changed from 'he' to 'we' by replacing يَ with نَ at the start. This is their response after قَالُوا (they said)

steps.m: idol, statue worshipped as a deity

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic noun meaning 'an idol'
Changes the word to show many idols by adding أ at the start and ا after ن

steps.m: idols (as the object being worshipped in نَعْبُدُ)
steps.n: Shows these idols are what is being worshipped in the phrase 'we worship idols'
Added fathas to connect the letters and show past tense meaning
Changed from past tense 'he remained' to present tense 'remain' by adding damma and combining the double ل with a shadda
Added نَ prefix to show 'we' are doing the action

steps.m: so we remain/stay (in response to نَعْبُدُ - we worship)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this is a result of the previous statement about worship
Added fatha to show the vowel sound on the letter lam
Added ها which shows this is happening to or for the idols (أَصْنَامًا) mentioned earlier in the verse. The ها is feminine because أَصْنَامًا is treated as feminine in Arabic.

steps.m: to stay, remain devoted to something

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters together

steps.m: one who stays devoted (subject of نَظَلُّ)
steps.n: Changed to active participle form showing who does the action. This describes the people who are remaining devoted to their idols.
Makes the word plural to refer to multiple people who are devoted to their idols

steps.m: those who stay devoted (plural, in accusative case)
steps.n: The fatha at the end shows this word is in the object position in the sentence

steps.m: he said (followed by a direct quote)
steps.n: Added fathas to show this happened in the past. The middle letter و changes to ا when between two fathas. This word introduces what someone said.
This is a question word that turns statements into yes/no questions. In this ayah, it's asking if the idols can hear when being called upon.
Added fatha on ه and sukun on ل to show correct pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
The يَ at the start shows this is happening now or in the future
The ون shows that many people (they) are doing it

steps.m: they hear/listen to you all (object of يَسْمَعُونَ)
steps.n: The كم shows that multiple people are being heard/listened to. This word appears in a question asking if idols can hear when being called upon.
This is a time word that connects two past events. In this ayah, it connects 'he said' (قَالَ) with what was said.
Added basic past tense vowels. The و changes to ا at the end because it's a weak letter.
Added تَ at start to show 'you' and changed vowels for present tense. The ا changes back to و.
Added نَ at the end to show this is addressing multiple people
Basic connecting word that gives a choice between 'they benefit you' or 'they harm' in this ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
The يَ at the start shows this is happening now, and the ُ ending shows this is the doer of the action
Adding ون shows this is about multiple people doing the action

steps.m: they benefit/help you all (object of أَوْ - 'or')
steps.n: كُمْ shows who receives the benefit/help. This word comes after أَوْ (or) in the ayah.
This is a connecting word that shows two possible options: either they benefit you OR they harm. It connects two verbs in this ayah.
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the letters
When two identical letters come together, they combine with a shadda
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels to damma
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action
Added fathas to connect the letters and make it past tense
Changed قَوَلَ to قَالَ by replacing the weak letter و with ا
Added وا to show multiple people did the action. This word is connected to يَفْعَلُونَ (they do) in the next part of the ayah, showing the same people are doing both actions.
This is a particle that introduces a correction or contrast to what was just said. Here it shows their answer is contradicting what was asked, like saying 'No, actually...'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense form
Changed the last vowel to sukun to prepare for adding the next suffix
Added نَا at the end to show 'we' did the action

steps.m: to care for/nurture as a father

steps.n: Root letters أ-ب-و become the simple noun 'father'
Changes from 'father' to 'fathers' by adding extra letters and stretching the middle sound
Adds نَا at the end to show possession ('our'). This word is what was found (object) in the phrase 'we found our fathers'
Starting with the basic demonstrative word ذا (this), we extend the alif to show distance
Added لِ to change from 'this' to 'that', showing more distance
Added كَ at the end to show even more distance

steps.m: like that (referring to how their ancestors acted)
steps.n: Added كَ at the beginning to mean 'like' or 'similar to'. This refers back to how their ancestors acted, mentioned in the previous part of the verse.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, putting sukun on first root letter, and damma on last root letter

steps.m: they do/act (referring to the idols mentioned in كَذٰلِكَ)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple subjects (the idols) are doing the action. This word completes the statement about what their ancestors found their idols doing.

steps.m: he said (introduces what Ibrahim said to his people)
steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense. This word introduces Ibrahim's speech to his people about what they worship.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in Arabic verbs with و in the middle
Changed from root letters to past tense 'he' form by adding vowel marks and using ى at the end
Changed from 'he saw' to 'I saw' by adding تُ at the end
Changed ending from تُ (I) to تُم (you all) to show many people did the action instead of one person
Added أَ at beginning to make it a question

steps.m: so have you all seen/understood? (connected to قَالَ)
steps.n: Added فَ to connect it to the previous word قَالَ (he said), and added hamza mark on alif
This is the basic form of the interrogative particle that asks about things
The shadda is added because this word connects to تَعْبُدُونَ (you worship). It asks 'what thing do you worship?'
Added vowels to make the basic past tense form
Command form - first vowel changed to 'u' sound and middle letter dropped to make it shorter and snappier
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did the action in the past (means 'I was')

steps.m: you all were (addressing multiple people in the past)
steps.n: Changed تُ (you) to تُمْ (you all) to show it's talking to many people instead of one person
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
The تَ at the start shows it's talking about 'you'
Adds plural masculine 'you' meaning to show multiple people worshipping
This is a standalone pronoun that doesn't come from a root. It's one of the basic building blocks of Arabic.
Adding م with sukun at the end makes it clear we're talking to multiple people

steps.m: to nurture, care for (like a father)

steps.n: The vowels make it a noun meaning 'father'
Adding كم at the end shows who the father belongs to - 'your father' when talking to many people
Adding آ at the start makes the word plural - changes from 'father' to 'fathers'
The ُ at the end shows this word is in the subject position

steps.m: and your fathers (subject of this phrase)
steps.n: Added وَ at beginning to connect this to the previous word أَنتُمْ (you)

steps.m: to come before, to be old

steps.n: Added أ at the beginning to show comparison, like saying 'more' or '-er' in English

steps.m: older/more ancient ones (referring to their ancestors)
steps.n: Added ون to show we're talking about many people

steps.m: the older/more ancient ones (referring to their ancestors)
steps.n: Added ال (written as ٱلْ) at the beginning to point to specific ancient ones - their ancestors mentioned in أَنتُمْ وَءَابَآؤُكُمُ (you and your forefathers)
Starting with the emphatic particle إِنَّ
Adding the conjunction particle فَ to connect with previous statement
Adding the attached pronoun هُمْ to complete the particle phrase

steps.m: to be hostile or exceed limits
steps.n: This root carries the basic idea of going beyond proper bounds or being hostile
The vowel pattern َ ُ turns this into a word describing someone who is always hostile
The double waw with tanween shows this is indefinite (like saying 'an enemy' instead of 'the enemy'). In this ayah, it's describing someone who is an enemy specifically to the speaker.
Adding shadda (ّ) to ل makes it stronger and adds emphasis, like saying 'surely' or 'definitely'

Adding ى at the end shows this means 'to me' specifically. This word connects to the next part of the ayah (إِلَّا رَبَّ ٱلْعٰلَمِينَ) to say 'surely to me [they are enemies] except the Lord of the worlds'
The alif (ٓ) at the end is just for pronunciation and doesn't change the meaning
This is a basic exception particle that shows something is different from what came before it. In this ayah, it's saying that everything mentioned before is an enemy EXCEPT what comes after.

steps.m: to care for and help grow
Lord (the one who cares for يَقُولُ)
steps.n: Connected letters with fatha and doubled middle letter to show intensity. This word is connected to يَقُولُ (he says) in this ayah.

steps.m: Lord (the one being addressed in يَقُولُ)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this is being directly spoken to, like saying 'O Lord!'
Changed from basic root meaning 'know' to a noun meaning 'sign' by adding the vowels a-a
Adding alif creates a noun meaning 'world' - something known/perceived
Added sound masculine plural ending to show multiple worlds
Added ال to make it definite. This word is possessed by رَبَّ (Lord) in the phrase رَبَّ العالمين (Lord of the worlds).
This is the base form of the relative pronoun that connects parts of a sentence, like 'who' or 'which' in English

steps.m: the one who/that which (subject of خَلَقَنِى - created me)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it more specific and definite. In this ayah, it refers to Allah who created the speaker.

steps.m: to create, form, or shape something
he created (connected to فَهُوَ يَهْدِينِ - so He guides me)
steps.n: Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. This word is connected to what comes after it in the ayah - 'so He guides me', showing Allah both created and guides.
Added نِى at the end to show that 'I' am the one being created. This is Ibrahim (peace be upon him) speaking about how Allah created him.
This is the basic pronoun meaning 'he' or 'it', used to point to someone or something previously mentioned (in this case, referring back to Allah who created)
Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce it correctly: هُ (hu) وَ (wa)

Added فَ at the beginning to connect this idea with what came before. It shows that this statement follows from the previous one about Allah creating. This pronoun 'he' refers to Allah who is the one doing the guiding in يَهْدِينِ (guides me)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb form
Changes from past tense (he guided) to present tense (he guides) by adding يَ at start
Added نِ at the end to show that 'me' is the object being guided (in this ayah, Ibrahim is saying that Allah guides him)

steps.m: the one who/that which (subject of يُطْعِمُنِى)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific. This word points to someone or something specific that will be described. Here it refers to Allah who feeds the speaker.

steps.m: and the one who/that which (subject of يُطْعِمُنِى)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it. This connects this description of Allah with previous descriptions.
Basic form of the third person masculine singular pronoun
Added marks to show this is 'he' as the doer of the action (the one who feeds)

steps.m: to feed or nourish with food

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Adding hamza at beginning makes the verb causative - showing someone causing the action to happen
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels
Added نِى at the end to show that 'me' is receiving the action of feeding
The basic meaning of giving someone something to drink
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb

steps.m: he gives drink (connected to يُطْعِمُنِى - he feeds me)
steps.n: Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start. This matches with يُطْعِمُنِى (he feeds me) in the ayah
Added نِ at the end to show the speaker is receiving the action
Added وَ at start to connect with previous phrase (يُطْعِمُنِى - he feeds me)
إِذَا is a time particle that introduces a condition or temporal clause. Here it introduces the condition 'when I became sick' (مَرِضْتُ).

steps.m: and when (connects to previous statement and introduces condition in مَرِضْتُ)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this condition with what came before it.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the past tense verb. This word is connected to يَشْفِينِ (he cures me) in the ayah.
Changed تُ at the end shows that 'I' did the action instead of 'he'. The sukun (ْ) is added before تُ to help pronunciation.
This is a basic pronoun meaning 'he' - one of the most common words in Arabic. Here it's the subject of the verb يَشْفِينِ (heals me).
The فَ prefix shows this is a result or consequence of what came before (being sick). The complete meaning in context is 'and when I am sick, so He (Allah) heals me'
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters and show past tense
Added يَ at the beginning to show present tense, and changed vowels to show ongoing action
Added نِ at the end to show that 'me' is receiving the healing. This word comes after هُوَ (He) in the ayah, showing that Allah is the one who cures.

steps.m: the one who/that/which (describes يُمِيتُنِى - causes me to die)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific. This word introduces a description of Allah, connecting to the next word يُمِيتُنِى (causes me to die).

steps.m: and the one who/that/which (describes يُمِيتُنِى - causes me to die)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this description to what was mentioned before it
Adding أ at the start makes the action causative - someone making the action happen to someone else
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels. The middle ا changes to ي in this form.
Added نِى to show that 'I' am the one being caused to die
This is the basic form of the word that shows something happens after something else

steps.m: then (after يُمِيتُنِى/he causes me to die)
steps.n: Added damma (ُ) on first letter and shadda with fatha (َّ) on last letter to show strong connection between events. In this ayah, it connects 'he causes me to die' with 'he gives me life'.
Adding vowels to make it a complete past tense verb meaning 'to live'
Added يُ at start to make it present tense and changed the pattern to make it mean 'causes/makes something to live' instead of just 'lives'
Added نِ at the end to show the action is being done to 'me'
Added kasra vowel mark to show basic form of the relative pronoun
Added ال with shadda to make it a specific relative pronoun

steps.m: and the one who/which (subject of أَطْمَعُ)
steps.n: Added و at beginning to connect it to what came before, and added elongation mark at end. This word is the subject of the verb أَطْمَعُ (I hope) in this ayah.
Added vowels (fatha) to make it past tense 'he hoped'

steps.m: I hope eagerly (for يَغْفِرَ - that He will forgive)
steps.n: Added أَ at beginning to show first person present tense, changed last vowel to ُ to show present tense, and added sukun on middle letter. This shows Ibrahim speaking about his current hope for Allah's forgiveness.
This is a connecting word that links أَطْمَعُ (I hope) to what is hoped for (forgiveness). It works like 'that' in English phrases like 'I hope that he forgives.'
Added vowels (َ) to make it past tense 'he forgave'
Changed to present/future tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Changed to subjunctive mood by adding fatha at end
Added kasra to show this is a preposition meaning 'for' or 'to'

steps.m: for me/to me (connected to يَغْفِرَ - forgive)
steps.n: Added ى at the end to show this means 'me'. This word is connected to يَغْفِرَ (forgive) in this ayah, showing who is being forgiven.

steps.m: to make a mistake or sin

steps.n: Root letters got vowels (fat-ha) to make a noun meaning 'mistake'
Changed pattern to show someone characterized by making mistakes
Added feminine ending to create noun for the sin itself
Added 'my' ending and changed ة to ت. This word is what Allah is being asked to forgive in the phrase 'that He will forgive me my sin'
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make it a noun. The و gets a sukun (ْ) because it's a weak letter.

Added fatha at the end because this word is connected to الدِّينِ (the judgment) to form the phrase 'the Day of Judgment'. It shows when the forgiveness mentioned in يَغْفِرَ (to forgive) will happen.
Root letters joined with kasra to make a noun meaning 'religion' or 'way of life'
Added shadda to emphasize the meaning
Added ال to specify this particular judgment/religion
Added kasra at the end because it shows this word is owned by/connected to يَوْمَ (making it 'Day of Judgment')

steps.m: to care for, raise, nurture completely

steps.n: Added يٰ at the beginning to show we're calling out to the Lord
Added kasra at the end to show we're calling out to our Lord
Connected the letters with fatha marks to make the past tense form
Changed to a command form by removing the first letter (و) and the last vowel (َ). This is a special change because و is a weak letter.

steps.m: grant! (to me) (connected to لِى after it)
steps.n: Added sukun mark to show where the word stops. This word is connected to لِى (to me) that comes after it in the ayah, showing who should receive the grant.
Added kasra to show this is a preposition meaning 'to' or 'for'
Added ى at the end to show this means 'to me'. This word is connected to هَبْ (grant), showing who is being granted something.
Changed the word from an action into the quality or ability itself
The ًا ending shows this is what is being granted. In this ayah, it's what is being asked for in the prayer 'My Lord, grant me wisdom'

steps.m: to join or catch up with

steps.n: Adding vowel marks to make a past tense verb
Changed from 'he joined' to 'you join!' by making it a command - added أ at start and changed the middle vowel from ـحِـ to ـحْـ
Added و at beginning to mean 'and'

steps.m: and join me! (as a command to Allah)
steps.n: Added نِى at the end to show the speaker is asking to be joined (with the righteous ones)

steps.m: to make something good or right

steps.n: Basic vowels (fatha) added to make it 'he' past tense
Changed pattern to show someone who has this quality
Added ين to make it plural (more than one)
Added ال to mean 'the' and doubled the ص with shadda
Added بِ meaning 'with' and final vowel mark
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he made' to 'make!' - turned into a command

steps.m: and make!/set! (command for me) (connected to لِّى after it)
steps.n: The و connects this to the previous request, while لِّى after it shows this is being requested for the speaker
Combined the preposition ل (for/to) with the pronoun ي (me/my). This shows who the action of اجْعَل (make) is being done for.
Added shadda to emphasize the ل. This is common when the preposition ل comes before pronouns.
Forms a noun for both the physical tongue and what it produces (speech/mention)
Added fatha at end to show this is the object of the verb اجْعَل (make/grant). In this ayah, Ibrahim is asking Allah to grant him good mention among later generations.
Changed the root letters into a noun by adding kasra (ِ) on first letter and sukun (ْ) on middle letter to show the concept of truth itself rather than the action of being truthful
Added tanwin kasra (ٍ) at the end to show this word is describing لِسَانَ (tongue/mention) in the phrase 'tongue/mention of truth', meaning a truthful reputation or good mention
This is a basic preposition that shows location or position. In this ayah, it shows that the good mention/reputation should be among the later generations.
The ي at the end is written as ى when it comes at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Changed the root into a word meaning 'later' by adding ءَا at the start and putting a kasra ( ِ ) in the middle - like saying 'more late' in English
Added ين to show we're talking about multiple people or things that come later
Added ٱلْ at the start to point to specific later ones, not just any later ones
Added َ at the end because this word is receiving the action of اجْعَل (make/grant) in the phrase 'grant me... among the later ones'

steps.m: to make or set something in a certain way

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed from 'he made' to a command 'make!' by adding alif at start and putting sukoon at end
Added ني at the end to show the command is being done to 'me'
Added و at start to connect this command to what came before it

This is a preposition that shows where something comes from or what it belongs to. In this ayah, it connects 'make me' (ٱجْعَلْنِى) with 'inheritors' (وَرَثَةِ), showing what Allah is being asked to make the speaker from/of.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the past tense verb
Changes from verb (inherited) to plural noun (inheritors)

steps.m: inheritors (of جَنَّةِ ٱلنَّعِيمِ - Garden of Pleasure)
steps.n: Added kasra to show this word is connected to what comes after it (Garden of Pleasure)

steps.m: to cover or hide with thick growth

steps.n: Added vowels and doubled the middle letter to show intense covering
Added ة to make it a noun meaning a place with lots of covering (like a garden full of trees)
Added ِ to show this garden belongs to or is connected to the word النَّعِيمِ (bliss) that comes after it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed middle letter sound and added ي to turn 'being comfortable' into 'complete comfort/bliss'
Added ال to specify a particular bliss, and doubled the ن with shadda

steps.m: of the perfect bliss (possessed by جَنَّةِ - garden)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show this bliss belongs to or is connected to the garden mentioned before it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed from 'he forgave' to 'forgive!' - a command asking someone to do it
Added 'and' at the beginning. This connects this command to previous statements in the prayer.

steps.m: to nurture, care for (like a father does)

steps.n: Root letters أ-ب-و became the simple noun 'father' by adding fatha
Added ي with kasra to show possession (my)

steps.m: to my father (object of وَٱغْفِرْ - forgive)
steps.n: Added لِ at beginning to show direction (to). This word is what is being forgiven in the phrase 'forgive my father'
This is an emphasis particle that makes you sure about what comes after it. Here it emphasizes that he (the father) was truly among those who went astray.
Added هُ which shows we're talking about 'he' (the father mentioned in لِأَبِىٓ). The small و (ۥ) is just to help with pronunciation.
Added fathas to show this happened in the past
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter in the past tense. This word is describing the father (أَبِى) in this ayah.
This is a preposition that shows where something comes from or what group it belongs to. In this ayah, it connects to ٱلضَّآلِّينَ (those who went astray) to show someone is from that group.
Added َ (fatha) at the end because it comes before a word starting with ال. This helps connect the words smoothly when reading.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb

steps.m: one who has lost the way (after مِنَ)
steps.n: Changes from a past tense verb (he lost) to a noun meaning someone who is lost. Like changing 'strayed' to 'stray person'.

steps.m: those who have lost the way (after مِنَ)
steps.n: Makes it plural (more than one). The word is in genitive case because it comes after the preposition مِنَ (from/of).
Adds 'the' (ال) at the start and makes it more specific - refers to those who are lost/misguided
This is the basic negation word in Arabic that makes something negative, like adding 'not' in English
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this negative statement to what came before it
This is the basic meaning that all words from this root share
Root letters got vowels to make past tense verb meaning 'he was disgraced'
Changed from being disgraced to causing disgrace to others
Changes from 'he' to 'she' by replacing يُ with تُ

steps.m: you disgrace me (in يَوْمَ يُبْعَثُونَ - the Day they are resurrected)
steps.n: Added نِى at the end to show that 'me' is the one being disgraced. This is part of a prayer asking not to be disgraced on the Day of Resurrection.
Connected the letters with vowel marks to form the basic noun. The و becomes silent with sukun.

steps.m: day (object of يُبْعَثُونَ - they are resurrected)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this word is receiving the action of resurrection mentioned later in يُبْعَثُونَ (they are resurrected). It means 'on the day when they are resurrected.'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to passive present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: they are being raised up (on the Day of Resurrection)
steps.n: Added ون to show this happens to multiple people. In this ayah, it refers to being resurrected on the Day of Judgment.
Passive verb meaning 'they are raised' - the ون ending shows it's about multiple people
Connected the letters with vowel marks. The middle letter و keeps its sound because it has sukoon. This word shows when the 'benefiting' happens.

steps.m: on the day (when wealth and children will not benefit)
steps.n: Adding fatha at the end shows this word tells us when something happens. It connects to the next part that talks about wealth and children not being helpful.
This is a negative particle that makes the verb after it (يَنفَعُ/benefits) not true. In this ayah, it's saying wealth will not benefit.
Added fatha mark on ل to show correct pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb

steps.m: he/it benefits/helps (in the ayah: wealth will not benefit)
steps.n: The يَ at the start shows it's happening in the present/future. The ُ at the end shows this is the main verb. In this ayah, it's talking about how wealth won't benefit anyone on the Day of Judgment.
Basic vowel marks (fatha and sukun) added to make the root letters readable as 'mal'
Added alif after first letter and tanween to make it a complete noun meaning wealth
This is a basic negation word that means 'not' or 'no'
Added و at the beginning to connect this negation with the previous one, creating a list of things that won't help on that day
Changed the middle and final vowels to create the singular noun 'son' from the verb
Added ن to make it plural. This word is one of the things that won't benefit on that day, along with مَالٌ (wealth)

steps.m: except (introduces exception to مَنْ أَتَى 'whoever comes')
steps.n: This is a fundamental particle that introduces exceptions. In this ayah, it excludes from a general group (whoever comes) those who meet a specific condition (with a sound heart). It has no derivational steps since it's not derived from a root.
This is a relative pronoun used to refer to people. In this ayah, it refers to the person who comes to Allah with a sound heart. It's connected to the verb أَتَى (came/comes) that follows it.

steps.m: he came (subject of bringing to اللَّهَ)
steps.n: Changed from basic root 'to come' into past tense 'he came' by adding vowel marks and changing the ending
Regular weak letter transformation for final ي in past tense verbs
Added ال to make it definite and specific, then merged letters according to Arabic rules. This makes it a proper noun referring to the One True God.
Added shadda to emphasize the L sound, fatha at the end to show Allah is the object being worshipped in this sentence, and changed initial alif to wasl form
The root meaning of turning/changing became associated with the heart since it can turn emotions. Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce it.
Added tanween (double kasra) to show this is any heart, not a specific one
Added بِ at the beginning to show this heart is being used as a tool or means
Added ي between second and third letters to show this is describing a quality someone has
This word describes قَلْبٍ (heart) in the phrase بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ (with a sound heart)
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Adding أ at the beginning makes the action affect something else
Changed vowels to show the action is being done to something rather than by something
Added feminine marker ت with kasra to match with الجنة (Paradise) which is feminine

steps.m: and it (feminine) was brought near (referring to الجنة)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect with previous statement

steps.m: to hide or cover something so it's protected

steps.n: Adding the vowel marks makes it a complete past tense verb
Added ة to make it a noun for a place that is covered/protected, which came to mean 'garden'
Added ٱلْ to make it specific and definite, referring to Paradise specifically

steps.m: Paradise (as the subject being brought near in this verse)
steps.n: Added ُ to show Paradise is the subject being brought near in وَأُزْلِفَتِ ٱلْجَنَّةُ (and Paradise is brought near)
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters

steps.m: one who guards themselves against evil
steps.n: Added مُ (mu) at start and ت (ta) after it, which merged with و (wa) to make تّ (tta), showing someone who protects themselves

steps.m: those who guard themselves against evil
steps.n: Added ين to make it plural

steps.m: the ones who guard themselves against evil
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific

steps.m: for the ones who guard themselves against evil
steps.n: Added لِ meaning 'for/to' and َ to show it's affected by the verb
Added vowels to make a past tense verb
Changed to passive voice and added emphasis with shadda

steps.m: it (feminine) was made to emerge (referring to الْجَحِيمُ)
steps.n: Added feminine marker ت and kasra ending to show this refers to Hell (الْجَحِيمُ) which is feminine

steps.m: and it (Hell) was made to emerge (referring to الْجَحِيمُ)
steps.n: Added conjunction و to connect this clause with what came before

steps.m: and it (feminine) was made to emerge
steps.n: Adds kasra for proper pronunciation
Added ي between second and third root letters to show this is a specific, intense form of fire - the hellfire itself
Adding ال makes it refer to the specific Hellfire mentioned in the Quran

steps.m: the Hellfire (subject of وَبُرِّزَتِ - 'and is made apparent')
steps.n: The damma shows this is the subject of the verb 'made apparent' in the ayah

steps.m: to stray from the right path

steps.n: Added alif after first root letter and vowels to show someone who does the action
Added ين to make it plural - means more than one person who strays
Added لِ at beginning to mean 'for' or 'to'

steps.m: for the people who stray (connected to الْجَحِيمُ/the Hellfire)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific. This word describes who the Hellfire (الْجَحِيمُ) is brought forth for.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks. The middle weak letter و becomes ا in this pattern.
Changed from active (he said) to passive (it was said) by changing the vowels

steps.m: and it was said (to them, as shown by لَهُمْ after)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to mean 'and'
Added fatha to show the vowel sound

steps.m: to/for them (who are being asked about their false gods in أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْبُدُونَ)
steps.n: Added هُمْ which means 'them' to show who the action is directed towards. This word is connected to the question that follows it, asking these people about where their false gods are.

steps.m: where (asking about the location of مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْبُدُونَ - what you used to worship)
steps.n: Basic question word asking about location. In this ayah, it's asking about the location of false deities that were worshipped.

steps.m: where (asking about the location of مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْبُدُونَ - what you used to worship)
steps.n: Added vowel marks to show exact pronunciation: fatha on أ (َ), sukun on ي (ْ), and fatha on ن (َ)
This is a question word asking about what they used to worship. It's connected to the verb تَعْبُدُونَ (you used to worship) that comes after it.
Changed from basic root to past tense by adding vowels
Changed from past tense 'was' to command form 'be!' by changing vowels

steps.m: you all were (before تَعْبُدُونَ - you all used to worship)
steps.n: Added تُمْ at the end to show 'you all' did something in the past
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
Changes 'he worshipped' to 'you worship' by adding تَ at the start
Makes the verb plural masculine
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from
This مِن works with the words after it (دُونِ ٱللَّهِ) to ask about false gods/helpers besides Allah

steps.m: to be beneath or less than something else

steps.n: Adding damma (ُ) changes it to mean 'beneath/less than'
The kasra is added because this word comes after مِن (from/of) in the ayah, which requires the following word to end in kasra
Added shadda to show emphasis of the ل
Added kasra at the end to show this word follows 'besides/other than' (دُونِ) in the phrase 'besides Allah'
Basic form of the question word without any vowel marks
Marks are added to show how to say it correctly - fatha (َ) on ha and sukun (ْ) on lam. Used here to ask 'Do they help you?' about false gods.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing middle vowel to ُ
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action
Added كُمْ to show who is being helped (you all)
Basic connecting word that gives a choice between two possibilities: 'help you' or 'help themselves'
Added fatha (َ) on أ and sukun (ْ) on و to show correct pronunciation
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing middle and end vowels
Added ت between first and second root letters to show the action is reflexive (done to oneself)
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action

steps.m: to throw or pile up repeatedly

steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowels to make a past tense verb. The doubled root shows the action happens multiple times.
Changed vowel pattern to show this happened to someone/something rather than they did it themselves

steps.m: and so was thrown down repeatedly
steps.n: Added فَ to show this happened as a result of something mentioned before

steps.m: and so they were thrown down repeatedly
steps.n: Added وا to show this happened to multiple people
This is a basic preposition that shows location or being inside something
Added the kasra vowel mark under ف to show how it's pronounced

steps.m: in it (referring to Hell mentioned earlier in the ayah)
steps.n: Added ها at the end to show this refers back to Hell (جهنم), which is feminine in Arabic
This is a standalone pronoun that refers to multiple people (all male or mixed group). In this ayah, it refers to those who were thrown into the fire.
Added vowels to connect the root letters together
This form shows who does the action. The ٍ (tanwin kasra) shows it's indefinite.
The ون ending makes it plural for males
ال makes it specific/definite

steps.m: and the ones who stray (subject of كُبْكِبُوا)
steps.n: Adds و meaning 'and' at the beginning. This word is the subject of the verb كُبْكِبُوا (they were thrown) in this ayah.

steps.m: to gather or assemble (like troops or armies)

steps.n: Root letters got vowels to make a noun meaning 'army' or 'group of soldiers'
Changed to broken plural form to show multiple groups of soldiers
Added وَ (and) at beginning and ُ at end to show this is the subject of a sentence
This is a proper noun borrowed from Greek 'diabolos' meaning 'slanderer'. It became the personal name for Satan in Islamic tradition.

steps.m: Iblis (as part of جُنُودُ إِبْلِيسَ - the armies of Iblis)
steps.n: Added a fatḥa at the end to show this word is connected to جُنُودُ (armies) in the phrase 'armies of Iblis'

steps.m: to gather or collect things together

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make it a past tense verb
Adding أ at start changes meaning to 'make something happen'

steps.m: all together (describing جُنُودُ إِبْلِيسَ - the armies of Iblis)
steps.n: Changed from a command 'gather!' to a word meaning 'all together' by adding ون at the end. Shows it's talking about many people (Iblis's armies) doing something together.
Added fatha (َ) after each letter to make the basic past tense form
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas, which is a common pattern in Arabic verbs

steps.m: they said (referring to the disbelievers arguing in Hell)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show many people did the action. This connects to the next verse where it mentions them arguing in Hell.
Added diacritics to show the correct pronunciation: هُمْ (hum). This is a pronoun meaning 'they'.

steps.m: and they (the ones disputing in فِيهَا يَخْتَصِمُونَ)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it. This و shows that this action is happening along with the previous statement.
Added kasra (ِ) to show the basic preposition meaning 'in'

steps.m: in it (feminine) (location where يَخْتَصِمُونَ/they dispute occurs)
steps.n: Added ها at the end to show this means 'in it'. The ها refers to something feminine that was mentioned before, and this is where the disputing (يَخْتَصِمُونَ) happens.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make basic past tense verb
Added اِ and تَ to show people arguing back and forth with each other
Changed from past to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels inside the word
Made plural by adding ون to show many people are arguing
This is the base word for Allah, God's proper name in Arabic
Added kasra at the end to show possession or connection
Added تَ at the start to make it into 'I swear by Allah' - this تَ is a special letter that turns Allah's name into an oath
This is an emphasis particle that makes the statement stronger. In this ayah, it emphasizes their admission of being in clear error, showing how certain they are about it now.
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: we were (subject of لَفِى ضَلٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ)
steps.n: Changed the word to show 'we' did the action by adding نَّا at the end. This word is connected to the phrase 'in clear error' in this ayah.
Added kasra to show it's a preposition meaning 'in'
Added لَ at the beginning to add emphasis. This word connects to ضَلٰلٍ (misguidance) in this ayah, showing they were surely in misguidance.

steps.m: to wander off the right path
he wandered off the right path
steps.n: Added fathas to connect the letters and make it a past tense verb
Changed from a verb (action) to a noun (concept) by adding alif after second letter

steps.m: in a state of wandering off the right path (after في/in)
steps.n: Added tanween kasr (ٍ) to show it comes after the word في (in). The alif is written as a small alif (ٰ) above the letter in this style.
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Added مُ at start and changed vowels to show something that does the action of making clear. This word describes the misguidance (ضَلٰلٍ) mentioned in the ayah.

steps.m: one who makes clear (in genitive case)
steps.n: Added ٍ at end to show this describes the word before it (ضَلٰلٍ/misguidance)
The shadda adds extra emphasis to show how clear/obvious something is

steps.m: when (connecting to نُسَوِّيكُم - we made you equal)
steps.n: This is a time particle that shows when something happened in the past. Here it connects to the next word نُسَوِّيكُم (we made you equal) to show when that action happened.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
The doubled middle letter makes the meaning more intense
Adding ن at the start shows that 'we' are doing the action

steps.m: we make you all equal (to رَبِّ ٱلْعٰلَمِينَ - Lord of the worlds)
steps.n: Adding كم suffix to indicate 'you all' as the object of the verb

steps.m: to care for, nurture, develop something to its full potential
lord (one who nurtures and takes care of others)
steps.n: The doubled middle letter shows this is someone who does this action perfectly. Adding fatha on first letter makes it a noun.

steps.m: by the Lord (connected to نُسَوِّيكُم - we made you equal)
steps.n: Adding بِ at the start shows how something was done. The kasra at the end connects it to the next word (ٱلْعٰلَمِينَ - of the worlds).
The root 'know' becomes 'world/realm' - something that can be known or experienced. This word is plural (worlds) and is owned by 'Lord' in the phrase 'Lord of the worlds'
Added ين to show there are many worlds/realms
Added ال to specify these particular worlds. This word is possessed by رَبِّ (Lord) in the phrase رَبِّ ٱلْعٰلَمِينَ (Lord of the worlds).
The word already has ال (the) in both forms - it means 'the worlds' and is owned by رَبِّ (Lord)

steps.m: what/that which (refers to the false deities they followed)
steps.n: This is a question word that shows confusion or denial about something. Here it's being used to ask about what led them astray.

steps.m: and what/that which (connects to previous statement about being misled)
steps.n: The و connects this question to their previous admission of being misled, showing they are explaining why it happened
The alif is lengthened to آ to emphasize the word and pause slightly when reading, which is common at the end of phrases in the Quran
Connected the root letters and added shadda to show the doubled ل
Adding أ at the start changes the meaning from 'going astray' to 'making someone else go astray' - like turning 'lost' into 'made others lost'

steps.m: he made us lose the way
steps.n: نا means 'us' - we are the ones being led astray. The نا is attached to the verb أضلّ as its direct object (the ones being led astray).

steps.m: he made us lose the way
steps.n: The final alif is lengthened for pronunciation emphasis at pause position

steps.m: except (excluding ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ from the previous statement)
steps.n: This is a complete particle that creates an exception - it tells us that what comes after it (ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ - the criminals) is excluded from what was mentioned before. In this ayah, it's saying that only the criminals led them astray.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past-tense verb
Added مُ at start and ِ in middle to show who does the action
ون makes it plural for masculine nouns
ال at the start makes it specific/definite
Added fatha to show proper pronunciation. This is a question word asking about something.

steps.m: so what/then what (connects to previous statement)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to connect this question to what came before it. In this ayah, it shows the question follows from a previous situation.
This is the basic pronoun meaning 'us' or 'our'
Added لَ at the beginning to show this is 'for us'. In this ayah, it's connected to شٰفِعِينَ (intercessors), showing we don't have any intercessors for us.
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects to شٰفِعِينَ (intercessors) to show we don't have any intercessors from anywhere.

steps.m: to help someone by speaking for them

steps.n: Added alif after first letter and vowels to show who does the action

steps.m: helpers who speak for others (after مِن/from)
steps.n: Added ين at the end to show there are many helpers. The word comes after مِن (from) in the ayah.
This is the basic negation word that says 'no' or 'not' to something
Added و at the beginning to connect this negation to what came before it
Shows someone being truthful in a specific situation
Added ي to show this is a strong or constant quality - someone who is always truthful becomes a close friend

steps.m: any close friend (in a possessive phrase with حَمِيمٍ)
steps.n: The word gets tanwin (two dots under) to show it's indefinite and part of the phrase 'no close friend', connecting it with another word (حَمِيمٍ)

The pattern with ي in the middle makes the meaning stronger, showing that something is very hot or boiling. In the Quran, this word specifically refers to extremely hot or boiling water used as a punishment.

steps.m: any boiling water (connected to وَلَا صَدِيقٍ - 'nor any friend')
steps.n: The tanwin kasra (ٍ) shows this is indefinite (any boiling water) and is connected grammatically to the previous phrase 'nor any friend'
This is the basic particle لَوْ which introduces hypothetical scenarios that didn't happen, like saying 'if only' in English.
Added فَ (and then/so) at the beginning to connect this wish to what came before it. Together فَلَوْ introduces a wish about something impossible, like saying 'oh if only' in English.
This is a basic particle that connects parts of a sentence, similar to how 'that' connects ideas in English

steps.m: that indeed (connects to فَلَوْ before it)
steps.n: The shadda (double letter mark) and fatha make this word stronger, showing emphasis. Here it comes after فَلَوْ (if only) to express a wish.
Added fatha to help with pronunciation

steps.m: for us (expressing a wish with فَلَوْ أَنَّ - 'if only')
steps.n: Added ل at the beginning to show this is being done 'for us'. In this ayah, it's part of expressing a wish - 'if only for us [there was] a return'
Added shadda to show the doubled middle letter, and added fatha on first letter
Added ة to make it refer to one instance of returning

steps.m: a return (object of أَنَّ لَنَا - that we have)
steps.n: Added tanween fatha to show this is the object of the phrase 'that we have' in the ayah
Added the basic vowel pattern to connect the letters

steps.m: we would be (responding to لَوْ - if)
steps.n: The ن prefix shows 'we' and the final vowel shows it's in response to 'if' (لَوْ). This is part of people wishing they could return to earth.

steps.m: so we would be (responding to لَوْ - if)
steps.n: The فَ shows this is the result of the 'if' statement - if we could return, then this would happen
This is a basic preposition that shows where something comes from or what group it belongs to
Added َ (fatha) at the end to help connect smoothly to the next word ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (the believers)

steps.m: to trust, believe in, feel safe with
steps.n: The root letters express the concept of safety and belief
Changes from verb 'to believe' into 'one who believes' by adding مُ prefix
Changed the ending to ين to show we're talking about many believers
Added ٱلْ at the start to mean 'the' and َ at the end because this word follows مِنَ (from) in the ayah
Adding shadda (ّ) changes this from a conditional word to an emphasis word. The shadda makes the 'n' sound stronger, which matches how it makes the meaning stronger.
This is the basic form of the preposition showing location or position. In this ayah, it connects 'sign' (ءَايَةً) with 'that' (ذٰلِكَ), meaning 'in that is a sign'
The ي is written as ى when it comes at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun used to point to something close by
The alif with bar on top (ٰ) changes 'this' to 'that', showing greater distance
Added لِ which helps connect the word parts together

steps.m: that (pointing to something far in the story)
steps.n: The كَ at the end makes it clear we're pointing to something mentioned in the story

steps.m: something that shows or makes clear
steps.n: The root letters combine with the first two letters merging into a long آ sound
The ة makes this a specific instance of something that shows or proves

steps.m: surely a sign (that proves the truth of what was mentioned before)
steps.n: The لَ adds emphasis meaning 'surely/indeed', and the ً (tanween) makes it indefinite. This sign refers back to the story mentioned in ذٰلِكَ (that) from the previous part of the verse.
This is a basic negative particle that makes statements negative, like adding 'not' in English. In this ayah, it's used to say that most of them were not believers.
Added و at the beginning to connect this statement with what came before. This connects to the verb كَانَ (was) to say 'and was not'.
Added short vowel marks (fatha) to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he was'
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter in past tense. This word tells us about أَكْثَرُهُم (most of them) in this ayah.
Added أ at beginning and vowel marks to make it comparative, like changing 'big' to 'bigger' in English
Added ُ to show this is the subject doing the action, then added هُم meaning 'them'

steps.m: to feel or give safety and trust

steps.n: Added مُ at the start to show who does the action, like changing 'trust' to 'one who trusts'
Added ين at the end to show we're talking about many male believers
Added shadda on م and fatha at the end to help with pronunciation when connecting with other words
This is an emphasis word that makes listeners pay attention to what comes next. The shadda (ّ) makes the ن sound stronger to show extra emphasis.

steps.m: and indeed (connects to رَبَّكَ - your Lord)
steps.n: The و at the start connects this phrase to what came before it. This word introduces the phrase 'and indeed your Lord' (وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ)
Added vowel marks to connect the letters together
The doubled middle letter (shadda) makes this an intensive form, emphasizing the complete nature of the nurturing
Added 'your' and marked it as being affected by إِنَّ (indeed/verily) which comes before it in the ayah
This is a basic pronoun that refers back to 'your Lord' (رَبَّكَ) mentioned earlier in the ayah

steps.m: surely He (referring to رَبَّكَ - your Lord)
steps.n: The لَ at the start adds emphasis because this is making a strong statement about Allah's attributes (العزيز الرحيم - the Mighty, the Merciful) that follow
Changed from an action to a description of someone who has this quality strongly

steps.m: The Mighty One (a name of Allah)
steps.n: Adding ال makes this a title/name rather than just a description
The ُ shows this is describing Allah in this ayah

steps.m: to be merciful, to show compassion
very merciful (one who shows intense mercy)
steps.n: Added ي between second and third root letters and vowels to show someone who has this quality very strongly

steps.m: The Most Merciful (one of Allah's names)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ to make it specific and definite, shadda for emphasis, and ُ to show it's describing Allah in this context
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb

steps.m: he strongly denied as false (subject of قَوْمُ)
steps.n: The doubled middle letter (shadda) makes the meaning more intense. This word is part of the subject قَوْمُ نُوحٍ (the people of Noah)

steps.m: she strongly denied as false (subject of قَوْمُ)
steps.n: Adding ت at the end makes it about a female (she) instead of a male (he)
Changes verb root into a noun meaning 'a group that stands together'
Adding the nominative case ending to mark the word's grammatical role
This is the name of the prophet Noah (peace be upon him), which doesn't come from a root
The ٍ shows that Noah is connected to قَوْمُ (people), making it mean 'people of Noah'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Added مُ at the beginning and changed vowels to show this is about who receives the action of sending
Added ين to make it plural, showing there are many messengers
Added ال to specify these particular messengers. This word is being acted upon by the verb كَذَّبَتْ (denied/rejected) in this ayah.
This is a time word that introduces a past event. In this ayah, it connects to when Nuh spoke to his people.
Added fatha marks to show this is past tense and the subject is 'he'
The middle letter و changes to ا because it has a fatha and comes between two fathas. This word introduces what نُوحٌ (Noah) said to his people.
Added fatha to show the basic preposition meaning
Added هُمْ to show who the action is directed towards. This word connects to قَالَ (he said) to show who was being spoken to.
Added fatha on first letter and damma on second letter to form the basic noun 'brother'
Added هُمْ at the end to show possession. This word is the subject (doer) of the verb قَالَ (said) in this ayah.
This is the basic form of the proper name Noah
The double damma (tanween) shows this is the subject doing the action of saying in this ayah. It connects to قَالَ (he said) which comes right after.
This particle is used to get attention, like saying 'Hey!' or 'Listen!' in English

steps.m: Will you not? (seeking attention before تَتَّقُونَ)
steps.n: Adding لا (meaning 'not') creates a questioning phrase that both gets attention and encourages the right action. Here it leads into تَتَّقُونَ (fear Allah), so it's like saying 'Hey, shouldn't you fear Allah?'
Changed from root letters to present tense 'he' form by adding ya- at start and vowels
Changed from 'he' to 'you' by replacing يَ with تَ at the start
Added تَّ to show you are doing the protecting/guarding to yourself (reflexive action)
Added ون to show multiple people are being addressed
This is a special word that adds emphasis to statements, like adding an exclamation mark in English

steps.m: indeed I (emphasizing the statement 'I am a trustworthy messenger' that follows)
steps.n: Added ي at the end to mean 'I/me/my'. The ن gets a kasra (ِ ) sound before this ending. This connects to the next words لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ (trustworthy messenger for you) to form the complete statement.
This is a preposition that shows something is being directed to someone. Here it connects رَسُولٌ (messenger) to who the messenger is for.
The كم at the end shows this is directed to multiple people being addressed. In the ayah, this connects to رَسُولٌ to show 'a messenger to/for you all'
This is the basic concept of sending or dispatching something

steps.m: messenger (someone whose job is sending messages)
steps.n: Changed the root 'send' into 'messenger' by adding vowels and و to make it mean 'someone who sends'
Added tanween to show this is indefinite (like saying 'a messenger' instead of 'the messenger'). This word is described by أَمِينٌ (trustworthy) in this ayah.
Added basic vowel marks to connect the root letters
Added ي to create an adjective describing a quality. This word describes رَسُولٌ (messenger) in the ayah.
Added tanwin (double damma) to show this is indefinite, matching رَسُولٌ which it describes
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added اِتَّ at the beginning to make it mean 'protect oneself' instead of 'protect others'
Added فَ at beginning to mean 'so/then'

steps.m: so protect yourselves! (command to a group)
steps.n: Changed from 'he protected himself' to 'you all protect yourselves' by adding وا at the end - makes it a command to many people
Changed the word to refer specifically to Allah by adding ال and merging letters

steps.m: Allah (as the object of فَٱتَّقُوا۟ - so fear/be mindful of)
steps.n: Added shadda to emphasize the ل, changed initial alif to wasl form, and added fatha at end to show this is the object of the verb 'fear/be mindful of'
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Changed to command form telling someone to obey
Added ون at the end to change from commanding one person to commanding a group of people
Added ِ (kasra) at the end to mean 'me' - showing who should be obeyed

steps.m: and all of you obey me!
steps.n: Added وَ (and) to connect this command with the previous one (فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ - so fear Allah)
This is a negative particle that negates what comes after it
Added vowel mark to show pronunciation
Changed the regular alif (ا) to a long alif (آ) - this is just a writing variation that means the same thing

steps.m: and not (used to negate what comes after in أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ - I ask you)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense verb
Changes to present tense first person (I) by adding أَ at start, making middle letter silent with sukun, and adding ُ at end

steps.m: I ask you all (object of asking in وَمَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ - and I do not ask you for any payment for it)
steps.n: Adds كُمْ at the end to show who is being asked. This word is part of a larger phrase saying 'I do not ask you for any payment for it'
This is the basic preposition meaning 'on' or 'upon'. It's one of the most common Arabic prepositions.
The ى changes to يْ when we're going to attach something to the end. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.
Added هِ at the end to show who or what is being referred to. هِ means 'him' or 'it' depending on context.
Basic preposition that in this context, combined with the negative before it (وَمَآ), means 'any'. It connects to the word أَجْرٍ (reward) after it.
Root letters joined together with vowels to make the noun meaning 'reward'
Added tanwin kasra to show this is the object of مِنْ (from) in the phrase 'from any reward'
Basic negative particle that negates the verb 'I ask you' in this verse. It's different from the conditional 'if' meaning of إِنْ that appears in other places.
Root letters arranged with fatha and sukun to make a noun meaning 'reward'
Added ي with kasra to show possession. This word is what is being asked about in the phrase 'I do not ask you for my reward'
Changed the letter shape at the end from ي to ى but keeps same meaning (my reward)
This is the base form of the exception particle. It has no root since it's a basic particle.
Added shadda and alif to emphasize the exception. In this ayah, it creates contrast with عَلَىٰ (upon) to emphasize that the reward is only upon Allah, not from anyone else.
Basic preposition showing something is on top of or responsible for something else
Added alif maksura (ىٰ) at the end to show this is the end of the word. In this ayah, it shows the reward/payment (أَجْر) that is being asked about.
Added fatha on first letter and combined the repeated ب with shadda to show emphasis. This changes the meaning from the action of nurturing to the one who does the nurturing.
Adding kasra at the end makes this a call/address to the Lord, like saying 'O my Lord!'

steps.m: to know, to mark, to distinguish

steps.n: Root meaning 'to know/mark' becomes a noun meaning a complete marked/known realm. Like how we mark borders around a world.
Adds masculine plural ending to show multiple worlds/realms

steps.m: the worlds, the realms (owned by رَبِّ)
steps.n: Adds definite article and shows this word is possessed by رَبِّ (Lord) in the phrase رَبِّ ٱلْعٰلَمِينَ (Lord of the worlds)
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a past tense verb
Added اِتَّ at the beginning to show doing the action to oneself. The middle letter gets a shadda (doubled) because it combines with the ت
Added فَ at beginning to mean 'so/then' and changed اِ to ٱ because it comes after فَ

steps.m: so protect yourselves! (command to a group)
steps.n: Changed to a command for multiple people by adding وا at the end and changing the vowels
Changed the word to the unique name for the One True God by adding ال and merging letters

steps.m: Allah (as the object of فَٱتَّقُوا۟ - so fear/be mindful of)
steps.n: Added shadda to show emphasis of ل, changed initial alif to wasl form, and added fatha at end to show this is the object of the verb 'fear/be mindful of'
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to command form telling someone to obey
Added و to show multiple people are being commanded
Added نِ to show I am the one being obeyed

steps.m: and all of you obey me!
steps.n: Added وَ to connect this command to the previous phrase (فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ)
Added short vowels (fat-ha) to make it mean 'he said' in the past
Changed middle weak letter و to ا, which is common for hollow verbs
Added وا to show multiple people did the action. This word is connected to the next word أَنُؤْمِنُ (should we believe) as they are speaking.
Added optional marks (alif khanjariyah and small high rounded zero) that don't change the meaning
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changes from past tense 'he' to present tense 'we' form. The ن (n) at the start shows it's 'we', and the hamza (ؤ) is added because the word starts with a vowel sound

steps.m: should we believe? (questioning the believers in قَالُوٓا۟)
steps.n: Adding أَ at the start makes it a question. This is being said in response to those who spoke (قَالُوٓا۟) in the ayah
Starting with the basic particle ل which shows direction or purpose

steps.m: for you/to you (being addressed by قَالُوٓا۟ - they said)
steps.n: Added كَ at the end to show this is directed to 'you' (one male). This word is connected to قَالُوٓا۟ (they said) as they are speaking to someone.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Added اِتّ at the beginning to make the meaning stronger - like following something very closely or carefully
Added كَ at the end to show who was followed

steps.m: and he followed you (referring to what the disbelievers said to Prophet Noah)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to connect this with the previous statement. The ا changes to ٱ after و
Connected the root letters with fatha to form basic past tense verb
Forms elative/comparative pattern meaning 'most X'
Adding ون makes it plural for a group of men

steps.m: the most despicable ones (referring to the followers of Prophet Noah)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it specific - here referring to the followers of Prophet Noah who were being mocked by the disbelievers
Added vowel marks to make it past tense and show who did the action
Changed و to ا because it's easier to say in Arabic
Added fatha to show this is the question word 'what' that is asking about knowledge in this ayah
Added و at the beginning to connect this question to the previous statement 'he said'
Changed the root into a noun by adding the specific vowel pattern that makes it mean the concept of knowledge itself

steps.m: my knowledge (subject of قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Adding ى with kasra (ِى) at the end shows this knowledge belongs to me. This word is being used as the subject in relation to قَالَ (he said) in this ayah.
This is a basic particle that means 'what'. In this ayah, it connects to the verb يَعْمَلُونَ (they do/used to do) to ask about their actions.

steps.m: with what (they used to do يَعْمَلُونَ)
steps.n: Added بِ at the beginning to show the meaning 'with'. This connects to the previous words قَالَ وَمَا عِلْمِى (He said: And what is my knowledge) to complete the thought 'with what they used to do'.

steps.m: to be or exist (in a state/condition)

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense form
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs
Added وا at the end to show multiple people (they) did this action in the past. This word is connected to يَعْمَلُونَ (they do/work) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense form
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, sukun on middle letter, and ُ at end
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action. This word describes what the people (they) were doing in the past, as indicated by كَانُوا (they were) in the ayah.
This is a negation particle that means 'not'. In this ayah, it's negating the word حِسَابُهُمْ (their account), saying 'their account is not except upon my Lord'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changes verb 'he calculated' into the noun 'calculation' by adding alif
Added damma at the end because this word will be connected to a possessive pronoun
Added هُمْ at the end to show this is their reckoning. This complete word is the subject of إِنْ in the ayah.

steps.m: except (what follows from what came before)
steps.n: This is a special word that shows something is different from what was just mentioned. In this ayah, it connects to عَلَىٰ رَبِّى (upon my Lord) to show that the reckoning is only upon Allah, excluding others.
This is a basic preposition that shows something is on top of, resting on, or dependent on something else
The alif at the end is written with a small mark (ٰ) to show it's being stretched in pronunciation. This word connects to رَبِّى (my Lord) that comes after it in the ayah.

steps.m: to care for, nurture, or develop something to completion

steps.n: The doubled middle letter (shadda) makes this a noun emphasizing complete nurturing and care
Adding ى at the end shows possession - 'my Lord'. This word is being addressed in relation to the verb تَشْعُرُونَ (you perceive/realize)
This particle introduces hypothetical situations that are unlikely or impossible

steps.m: if (hypothetical, used with تَشْعُرُونَ - you perceive)
steps.n: Added vowel marks to show exact pronunciation. In this ayah, it introduces a hypothetical situation related to the verb تَشْعُرُونَ (you perceive/understand).
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Removed first vowel and changed middle/end vowels to 'u' sound to make present tense
Added تَ to show 'you' are doing the action
Added ونَ to show multiple people are doing the action
This is a very common negative particle in Arabic

steps.m: and not (negates أَنَا۠ بِطَارِدِ - I am a driver away)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this with what came before it. The آ at the end makes the sound longer for emphasis. This word negates the phrase that follows it (أَنَا۠ بِطَارِدِ - I am a driver away).
This is one of the basic pronouns in Arabic that isn't built from a root - it's a complete word by itself that means 'I'
Adding alif at the end makes the pronoun stronger and more clear
The small circle mark (۠) shows this is a special pronunciation that makes the word even stronger
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
The ا after the first root letter makes this describe someone who does the action. The word is in genitive case because it comes after the preposition ب.

steps.m: with/by one who drives out (connected to أَنَا۠ - I am)
steps.n: The preposition بِ is added to show 'with/by'. This word connects to أَنَا۠ (I am) to say 'I am not one who drives away...'
Adding vowels makes this a past tense verb about feeling secure
Added مُ at start to make it mean 'one who' does the action, with أ changing to ؤ after مُ
Added ين to show there are many believers

steps.m: the believers (object of بِطَارِدِ - one who drives away)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ to specify these particular believers, and َ at the end to show this word is receiving the action of بِطَارِدِ (one who drives away)
This is a negative particle that means 'not' in this context. While إِنْ can mean 'if' in other contexts, here it's being used with the meaning of ما (not) to negate what follows.
This is a basic pronoun meaning 'I'. In this ayah, it's connected to نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ (clear warner) to mean 'I am only a clear warner'.
Added a small alif (۠) above the final alif. This is a pronunciation mark that doesn't change the meaning but helps with proper recitation.

This is the base form of the word that shows something is being excluded from what came before it. In this ayah, it connects to the previous statement 'I am not' (إِنْ أَنَا۠) to mean 'I am not... except'

steps.m: except/only (connecting إِنْ أَنَا۠ with نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ)
steps.n: The shadda and alif are added to complete the word. In this ayah, it connects 'I am not' with 'a clear warner' to mean 'I am not anything except a clear warner'
Added basic vowel marks to connect the root letters
Added ي between second and third root letters to show this is someone who does the warning as their role
Added tanween damma to show this is indefinite (a warner). This word follows إِلَّا (except) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Added مُ at start and kasra on middle letter to show this describes someone/something that does the action
Added shadda to emphasize the strength of the clearing/explaining
Added tanween to show this is indefinite (like 'a' or 'an' in English)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
The middle letter و changed to ا which is common for hollow verbs
The suffix وا changes the subject from 'he' to 'they (masculine)'. This word is connected to what follows, where the disbelievers threaten Noah.
This is the basic conditional particle that shows something depends on something else

steps.m: if indeed (introducing the condition in قَالُوا)
steps.n: Added لَ at the beginning to add emphasis. This emphasizes the condition being stated after قَالُوا (they said).
