This is a negative word that makes something false in the past. In this ayah, it negates تَنتَهِ (you stop/desist), making it mean 'you do not stop.'
The shadda (ّ) is added because this word comes after لَئِن (if), making the 'not' stronger and more emphatic.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Added تَ at start to mean 'you', and turned it into present tense by adding نْ and making it reflexive (doing to yourself)

steps.m: you stop (in the context of 'if you don't stop')
steps.n: The ي at the end is dropped and replaced with a silent mark (sukun) because it comes after 'if not' (لَمْ) which changes how the verb ends
Added damma marks to show this is the main subject of what's being said

steps.m: O Noah! (being called by his people)
steps.n: Added يٰ at the beginning to show someone is calling out to Noah. This is used in the Quran when directly addressing someone.
Changed the root letters to make a present tense verb by adding vowels
Added تَ to show this is about 'you'
Added لَ to show emphasis and future meaning

steps.m: you will most certainly be (threatened by قَالُوا)
steps.n: Added نَّ for extra emphasis. This is part of a threat made by the people to Prophet Nuh.
Basic preposition that shows something comes from or is part of something else
Added َ (fatha) at the end to connect smoothly with the next word الْمَرْجُومِينَ (the stoned ones). Together they mean 'among the stoned ones'
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
Changed from 'he stoned' to 'person who gets stoned' - like changing from thrower to target
Added ال to make it specific

steps.m: the stoned ones (plural, object of مِنَ)
steps.n: Added ينَ to show this refers to many people and is the object of the word مِنَ (from/among) in the ayah
Added vowel marks to make the past tense form. This word is connected to كَذَّبُونِ (they denied) in the ayah, showing what he said before they denied him.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs (verbs with و or ي in middle)
Doubling the ب adds emphasis to show complete/perfect lordship

steps.m: O my Lord! (speaking to Allah in قَالَ)
steps.n: The kasra ending also shows possession - 'my Lord'. This word appears after قَالَ (he said), showing someone is directly speaking to Allah.
This is the basic form of the word that shows something depends on something else
Adding the shadda makes this word emphasize what comes after it. Here it emphasizes how the speaker's people denied him.
Changes verb root into a noun meaning 'a group that stands together'
Added ى with kasra (ِ) to show possession meaning 'my'
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make it a past tense verb
Doubling the middle letter makes the meaning more intense
Changed from singular (he) to plural (they)

steps.m: they denied me (object of قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Added نِ at the end to show that 'me' is the object being denied. This word is the object of قَالَ (he said) in this ayah.
Added short 'a' vowels (fatHa) to make it 'he opened' in past tense
Changed from 'he opened' to 'open!' by adding alif at start and making the end silent - this makes it a command
Added فَ (so/then) at beginning to connect to previous context. The alif gets the hamza-wasl mark since it follows another word.

steps.m: to separate or be distinct from

steps.n: Changes from root meaning to a noun meaning 'between' or 'among'
Added ى at the end to show this means 'me'. This word pairs with وَبَيْنَهُمْ (and between them) in the ayah to show the space between two groups.
Added vowel marks to make it a preposition showing location between things
Adding هم means 'them' (for a group of males)
Adding وَ connects this phrase to the previous بَيْنِى (between me) in the ayah, showing 'between me and between them'
The root letters ف-ت-ح become a noun فَتْح meaning 'judgment'. It's what someone is asking for when they say 'judge between us'.
Added tanween alif to show this is the direct object of the verb فَٱفْتَحْ (judge/decide).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Adding shadda makes the command more emphatic/intense
Added نِي at the end to show the command is to save 'me'
Added و at beginning to connect this command with what came before it
This is a relative pronoun used to refer to people. Here it connects to مَّعِىَ (with me) to mean 'whoever is with me'
Adding وَ at the start connects this to the previous phrase, joining 'save me and whoever is with me'
This is a preposition meaning 'with' that shows accompaniment
Added vowel marks to show how the word is pronounced
Added ي at the end to show this means 'with me'
Added shadda on first letter for emphasis and connection with previous word
Changed the final ي to ى with fatha for proper writing style
Basic preposition meaning 'from' or 'of'. In this ayah, it connects 'save me' (نَجِّنِى) with 'the believers' (ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ), showing who should be saved.
Added fatha at the end to connect smoothly with the next word (ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ). This is a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning.
Added مُ at the start and changed middle letter to ؤ to make it mean 'someone who believes/trusts'
Added ين at the end to show there are many believers
Added ٱلْ at the start to point to specific believers and َ at the end because it comes after مِنَ in the ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Adding أ at the start makes it mean 'to make someone do the action' instead of just doing it yourself
Adding نَا changes the subject from 'he' to 'we'
Adding فَ shows this action happened as a result of something before it
Adding هُ shows who was saved (him)
This is a relative pronoun that means 'who' or 'whoever'

steps.m: and who (connected to فَأَنجَيْنٰهُ - so We saved him)
steps.n: Added و with fatha at the start to mean 'and'. This connects back to the previous phrase 'so We saved him', showing who else was saved.
This is a preposition that means 'with' or 'along with'
Added fatha marks to show correct pronunciation
The م gets a shadda (double letter) because it comes after و, making it blend together smoothly
Added the pronoun هُ (him) to show who is being referred to (refers to Noah in this ayah)

steps.m: with him (those who were with Noah)
steps.n: Added the elongation mark ۥ to show proper pronunciation of the final هُ
This is a basic preposition that shows location or being inside something. It's not made from a root - it's one of the basic building blocks of Arabic.
Connected the letters together to make the basic word
Added the kasra (ِ) mark under the ف to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'fee'

steps.m: in/within (connects فُلْك to what comes before it)
steps.n: Changed the ي to ى which is just a different way of writing the same sound. This word connects فُلْك (ship) to the previous part of the sentence.

steps.m: to move in a circular path

steps.n: Added ُ (damma) on first letter and ْ (sukun) on second letter to create the noun 'ship'. This pattern is used to make nouns from actions - here possibly because ships move in curves through water.
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific
Added ِ (kasra) at the end because it follows فِى (in) in the ayah
Added vowels to connect the root letters
Changed to show the state of being filled rather than the action of filling
Added ال to show this refers to a specific loaded thing (the ship) mentioned in the ayah
Added kasra at end to show this word follows and describes 'the ship' (الفلك)
Basic form of the word that shows something happens after something else
Marks added to show this word connects actions in order - like saying 'first this happened, then that happened'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Adding أَ at the start makes the verb causative - someone is causing the drowning to happen to others
Adding نَا at the end shows that 'we' did the action
The root meaning relates to distance, either in space or time
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to form the basic noun meaning 'after'
Added damma at the end because this word comes after ثُمَّ (then) in the ayah, showing it's the subject of what happens next
The root meaning is about something continuing or remaining
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed the word to show who does the action (like changing 'stay' to 'stayer')

steps.m: those who stay/remain (acted upon by أَغْرَقْنَا - we drowned)
steps.n: Added ين to make it plural and show it's being acted upon by the verb 'we drowned'

steps.m: the ones who remain (acted upon by أَغْرَقْنَا - we drowned)
steps.n: Added ال to point to specific people who remain
This is a special word that adds emphasis to what comes after it. The shadda (ّ) makes the emphasis stronger, like saying 'most certainly' instead of just 'certainly'.
This is a basic preposition showing that something exists within or as part of something else. Here it connects 'sign' (ءَايَةً) with 'that' (ذٰلِكَ), showing the sign exists in/within that event.
The ي at the end is written as ى when it's at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Added the kasra (ِ) mark under ف to show it's pronounced with an 'i' sound, like in 'in'
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun used to point to something close by
The alif with bar on top (ٰ) changes 'this' to 'that', showing greater distance
Added لِ which helps connect the word parts together

steps.m: that (pointing to something far in the story being told)
steps.n: The كَ at the end makes it clear we're pointing to something mentioned in the story
The root letters combine with vowels to form the base word. The hamza and alif combine to form a long آ sound.
The ة shows this is one specific instance of a sign or miracle

steps.m: surely a sign/miracle (emphasizing the truth of what came before in ذٰلِكَ)
steps.n: The لَ adds emphasis meaning 'surely/indeed', while ً (tanween) shows this is indefinite like 'a sign'. This word emphasizes that what was mentioned before (ذٰلِكَ) is definitely a sign.
This is a negative particle that negates what comes after it. In this ayah, it negates كَانَ (was) to mean 'was not'.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it. This connects to the previous ayah which mentioned a sign/miracle.
Added vowel marks (fathas) to change from the root meaning 'to be' into 'he was' in the past tense
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs (verbs with و or ي in middle)
Added أ at beginning to make comparative form meaning 'more' or superlative form meaning 'most'
Added هُم at the end to show possession or reference to 'them'
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a past tense verb
Changed from 'believed' (past action) to 'believer' (person who believes)
Forms regular masculine plural from singular مؤمن
The doubled م adds extra emphasis to show strong belief
This is an emphasis word that makes listeners pay attention to what comes next. The shadda (double letter mark) makes the emphasis stronger.

steps.m: and indeed (connects to رَبَّكَ - your Lord)
steps.n: The و at the start connects this with what came before. This word emphasizes the statement about 'your Lord' (رَبَّكَ) that follows.

steps.m: Lord (one who cares for and develops)
steps.n: Added shadda (ّ) to show this is someone who does a lot of caring and developing
Added كَ to show it belongs to 'you'. The word comes after إِنَّ (indeed/certainly) which emphasizes it
This is a basic pronoun meaning 'he', referring back to 'your Lord' mentioned just before

steps.m: surely He is (emphasizing that your Lord is العَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ)
steps.n: The لَ at the start adds emphasis because this is part of إِنَّ...لَ construction, which strongly emphasizes the following statement about Allah being the Mighty, the Merciful
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Changed to pattern فَعِيل to show this is a strong quality

steps.m: The Mighty One (one of Allah's names)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it refer to a specific mighty one - in this case it's one of Allah's names
Adding ُ shows this is describing Allah in this ayah

steps.m: to be merciful, to show kindness

steps.n: Added ي to show this describes someone who has lots of mercy, like changing 'mercy' to 'merciful'

steps.m: The Extremely Merciful One (one of Allah's names)
steps.n: Added ال to show we're talking about THE Most Merciful - this is one of Allah's special names

steps.m: The Extremely Merciful One (one of Allah's names, describing Allah in this ayah)
steps.n: The ُ shows this word is describing Allah in this sentence
Doubling of middle root letter shows the denial was strong or emphatic. This word tells what عَادٌ (the people of 'Ad) did.
Adding feminine تْ shows the subject is feminine (here referring to عَادٌ which is a feminine word even though it refers to a group of people)

steps.m: to return or come back repeatedly

steps.n: Adding ا makes it a past tense verb
The word 'Aad' (name of an ancient people) gets two dots on top to show it's doing the action in the sentence
Added مُ at the start and changed vowels to show this is someone who receives the action of being sent
Added kasra at the end to show this word is being possessed by something
Added ين to show we're talking about multiple messengers
Added ٱلْ to show these are specific, known messengers

steps.m: the messengers (who عَادٌ/the people of 'Ad denied)
steps.n: Added َ to show the messengers are receiving the action of denial from عَادٌ (the people of 'Ad)

steps.m: when (introducing the time of هُودٌ speaking to his people)
steps.n: This is a time particle that connects two events in the past. Here it introduces when Hud spoke to his people.
This is the three-letter root that means speaking or saying
Added vowel marks (fatha) to make it mean 'he said' in the past tense
Changed قَوَلَ to قَالَ by replacing the weak letter و with ا
Added fatha to show the basic preposition meaning

steps.m: to/for them (who هُودٌ is speaking to)
steps.n: Added هُمْ to show who is being referred to. In this ayah, Hud is speaking to his people, so 'them' refers to his people.
This root relates to brotherhood and fraternal relationships
The ُ (damma) at the end shows this is in the nominative case, like when 'brother' is the subject of a sentence
Adds هُمْ at the end to show the brother belongs to them (referring to Hud's people)
This is the name of a prophet mentioned in the Quran
Added damma to show this is the subject (doer) of the verb قَالَ (he said) in this ayah
Added double damma to show this is an indefinite proper name
This is a question particle that turns a statement into a question

steps.m: Will you not? (encouraging هُودٌ's people to do تَتَّقُونَ)
steps.n: Adding لا (not) to the question particle creates a rhetorical question that encourages action. Here, Hud is encouraging his people to be righteous.

steps.m: to protect or guard against harm
steps.n: This root relates to protection and being mindful
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
Added ت at start to show 'you' or 'she' as the doer
Added another ت and merged it with the middle letter to show doing the action to yourself
Added ون at the end to show many people are doing it
This is a special word that adds emphasis to statements, like adding an exclamation mark in English

steps.m: indeed I (emphasizing رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ - a trustworthy messenger)
steps.n: Added ي at the end to mean 'I/me/my'. The ن gets a kasra (ِ ) sound before this ending. This connects to the next words which tell what 'I' am - a trustworthy messenger.
This is a preposition that shows something is being given or directed to someone. Here it connects to رَسُولٌ (messenger) to show who the messenger is for.
Added كم at the end to show this is directed to multiple people being spoken to. The full phrase لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ means 'a messenger to/for you all'.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create basic past tense verb
Changes from a verb (sending) to a noun (person who sends/delivers messages)
Added double-damma (tanween) to show this is referring to an unspecified messenger. In this ayah, it's describing a messenger sent to the people of 'Ad.
Added basic vowel marks to connect the root letters
Added ي to show this is a lasting quality
Added tanwin (double damma) to show this is an adjective describing the messenger mentioned in the previous word رَسُولٌ
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Added اِتَّ at the beginning to make it mean 'protect oneself' instead of 'protect others'
Changed from 'he protected himself' to a command telling multiple people to protect themselves by adding وا at the end

steps.m: so protect yourselves (plural command) (connected to أَطِيعُونِ)
steps.n: Added فَ (so/then) at the beginning to connect this command with the following command to obey (أَطِيعُونِ)
Changed the word to refer specifically to Allah by adding ال and merging letters

steps.m: Allah (as the object of فَٱتَّقُوا۟ - so fear/be mindful of)
steps.n: Added shadda to emphasize the ل, changed initial alif to wasl form, and added fatha at end to show this is the object of 'fear/be mindful of'
Changed root letters into a command meaning 'obey!' by adding kasra. The و drops out because it's a weak letter between two vowels.
Added أَ at beginning to make it a command form
Added ون to make it a command to multiple people
Added a small line (kasra) at the end to show you're talking to multiple people and asking them to obey you
Added وَ at beginning to connect this command with previous phrase فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ (so fear Allah)
Added fatha to show correct pronunciation
Changed the regular alif (ا) to a long alif (آ) - this is just a writing variation that means the same thing

steps.m: and not (negates أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ - I ask you)
steps.n: Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before. This word negates the verb أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ (I ask you) that comes after it.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Changed to present tense first person by adding أَ at start, putting sukun on س, and adding ُ at the end
Added كُمْ at the end to show who is being asked (you all)
This is the basic preposition meaning 'on' or 'upon'. It's one of the most common Arabic prepositions.
The ى changes to يْ when we're going to attach something to the end. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.
Added هِ at the end to show who or what is being referred to. هِ means 'him' or 'it' depending on context.
Basic preposition showing where something comes from or what it's made of. Here it connects 'reward' (أَجْرٍ) to 'I ask you' (أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ), showing what kind of reward is being asked about.
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to change from action to the thing itself (the payment/reward)
Added tanwin kasra to show this is any payment/reward being discussed in the sentence. This word is part of a phrase meaning 'I do not ask you for any payment'
This is a negative particle that negates the verb أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ (I ask you) in this context. While إِنْ can mean 'if' in some cases, here it means 'not' because it's negating a present tense verb.

steps.m: to give payment or reward for work

steps.n: Root letters turned into a noun meaning 'reward' by adding the vowels a-j-r
Added kasra to connect with the coming ي
Added ى with fatha to show the reward belongs to me
This is the base form of the exception particle

steps.m: except (excluding what comes after from what was mentioned before)
steps.n: Adding shadda and alif makes the exception meaning stronger and clearer. In this ayah, it's excluding the speaker's reward from what they're asking from their people - they want no reward except from Allah.
Basic preposition indicating position or responsibility. The ى with alif above is just a writing variant
Added vowel marks to show pronunciation: fatha on ع and ل
Added the small alif (ٰ) above the ى. This is just a writing variant that doesn't change the meaning. This word connects to رَبِّ (Lord) in the ayah.

steps.m: to care for, nurture, develop something to its completion

steps.n: Doubling the middle letter ب with shadda and adding fatha creates a noun meaning the one who does this action perfectly
Adding kasra at the end shows this word is connected to what comes after it (العٰلَمِين/the worlds)
Changed the root 'know' into a noun 'world' by adding an alif after ع and vowels - like how the world contains all things to know
Added ين at the end to show multiple worlds. This word is in the genitive case because it comes after رَبِّ (Lord), showing that these worlds belong to the Lord.
Added ال at beginning to make it specific. This word is part of the phrase رَبِّ ٱلْعَالَمِين meaning 'Lord of the worlds'.
Added fatha at the end to show this word is in the accusative case
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changes to present tense by adding تَ at start and changing vowels
Adds أَ at beginning to make it a question
Adds ون to show multiple people are being addressed
Added damma and shadda to make it mean 'every' or 'all'
Added kasra to show this word is connected to رِيعٍ (high place) that comes after it
Added بِ at the beginning to show 'with' or 'by'. This word is part of the question أَتَبْنُونَ بِكُلِّ رِيعٍ (Do you build on every high place?)

steps.m: to rise up or be elevated

steps.n: Changes the action of rising into a noun for a high place

steps.m: any high place (in this ayah: describing where people build)
steps.n: Adds tanween kasratayn to show this is referring to any high place, not a specific one

steps.m: to show or point to something

steps.n: Adding ة at the end turns this into a noun meaning 'sign'
Adding tanween (double mark) makes it indefinite and shows it's being used as an object

steps.m: a sign (object of تَبْنُونَ - you build)
steps.n: The ً (tanween) shows this is indefinite (any sign) and is being acted upon by the verb تَبْنُونَ (you build)
This root carries the meaning of doing something without purpose or benefit
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Changed from 'he' to 'you' by replacing يَ with تَ
Added ونَ to show multiple people are doing the action
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed form to show doing the action for oneself. The initial alif is dropped when adding تَّ.
Added تَ at beginning to show 'you' and ُ at end to complete the present tense - this makes it mean 'you take' instead of just 'take'
Added ونَ to show multiple people doing the action

steps.m: and you all take for yourselves (connected to مَصَانِعَ)
steps.n: Added وَ to connect this verb with the word مَصَانِعَ (structures/fortresses) that follows it
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Added مَ at beginning to create a noun meaning 'place where something is made'
The singular word changes to plural by adding an alif after م and a kasra (i) before ع
Added fatha at end to show this is the object of the verb تَتَّخِذُونَ (you take/adopt)
This is a word that shows hope or expectation for something to happen. Here it connects to the verb تَخْلُدُونَ (you will live forever)

steps.m: so that you all might (live forever)
steps.n: Adding كُمْ shows this is addressing multiple people (you all)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Adding ت at the start and changing vowels makes it 'you' in the present tense instead of 'he' in the past

steps.m: you (all) last forever (addressing the people in the ayah)
steps.n: The ون ending shows this is addressing multiple people. In this ayah, it's addressing the people who are building grand palaces hoping to live forever.
This is a compound word made up of two parts: و (and) + إذا (when)

steps.m: to seize forcefully or attack violently

steps.n: Added fathas to connect the letters and make the past tense form
Changed the last vowel to sukun to prepare for adding a suffix

steps.m: you all seized forcefully (describing how you acted as جَبَّارِينَ/tyrants)
steps.n: Added تُم at the end to show that many people (you all) did this action. This connects to جَبَّارِينَ (tyrants) in the ayah, showing how they acted tyrannically.
Connected the letters together and added fatha marks to show this happened in the past
Changed the last vowel to sukun to prepare for adding the next part
Added تُم at the end to show that multiple people (you all) did this action in the past
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
The shadda and alif make it mean someone who does this action intensely or habitually

steps.m: tyrants/oppressors (in this ayah: describing how people act when they strike others)
steps.n: The ين ending makes it plural and shows these people are being described in the sentence
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Adding تّ changes meaning to doing the action to/for oneself
Added فَ at beginning to mean 'so/then'

steps.m: so protect yourselves! (command to a group)
steps.n: Changed from 'he protected himself' to 'you all protect yourselves!' by adding وا to make it a command for many people
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. This word is unique and always refers to The One True God in Islam.

steps.m: Allah (as the object of فَٱتَّقُوا۟ - so fear/be mindful of)
steps.n: Added shadda on ل, changed initial alif to wasl form, and added fatha at end to show this is the object of the verb 'fear/be mindful of'
Changed root letters into past tense verb by adding the vowel 'aa' in middle and 'a' at end
Changed to command form telling someone to obey
Added plural ending to address multiple people
Added نِ to show who is being obeyed (me)

steps.m: and all of you obey me!
steps.n: Added وَ (and) to connect this command with previous ones
Added vowels to root letters to make past tense 'he protected'
Added Form VIII pattern (ا + ت with shadda) to show doing action to oneself
Changed to command form for multiple people

steps.m: and protect yourselves (command to اَلَّذِي who provided you)
steps.n: Added 'and' at beginning and connected it to next ayah which explains who they should be mindful of
This is the basic form of the word that connects descriptions to things, like saying 'who' or 'which' in English
Adding ال makes it more specific, like changing 'who' to 'the one who'

steps.m: who, which (connects to أَمَدَّكُم - provided you)
steps.n: Final spelling adjustments: The alif at the start becomes ٱ, the ل gets a shadda (ّ), and the ي becomes ى with a small alif (ٓ) above it. This word connects to أَمَدَّكُم (provided you) to tell us who did the providing.
Connected the root letters and doubled the middle letter to show basic past tense meaning
Adds 'أ' at start to mean 'made someone/something extend' or 'provided to others'
كم means 'you all' - the ones receiving the action
Added kasra (ِ) to connect it to the next word. This particle shows how something is done.
Added ما (what) to specify what is being referred to. This combination connects to the verb تَعْلَمُونَ (you know) to mean 'with/by what you know'.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changes from 'he knew' in past to 'you know' in present by adding تَ at start
Adds ون to make 'you know' plural (talking to many people instead of one)
The root letters م-د-د were joined together and one د was combined with the other to make مَدَّ with a shadda mark
Adding أَ at the start shows that he is doing this action to benefit others

steps.m: he provided you all (with بِأَنْعٰمٍ وَبَنِينَ - livestock and children)
steps.n: Adding كُم shows who received the provision. This connects to the next words that tell what was provided: livestock and children
Root meaning 'blessing' becomes a noun meaning 'livestock' - since animals were seen as a blessing from God
Changes from single animal to plural form meaning 'groups of livestock/cattle'

steps.m: with livestock (connected to أَمَدَّكُم - He provided you)
steps.n: Adds بِ meaning 'with' and tanween showing indefiniteness. This word shows what Allah provided to the people.
Added vowels to connect the letters and make the basic word for 'sons'

steps.m: sons (plural, object of أَمَدَّكُم - He provided you with)
steps.n: Added ين to make it plural and show it's receiving the action (what was provided). بَنِي is already plural, so this is just changing its form.

steps.m: and sons (plural, object of أَمَدَّكُم - He provided you with)
steps.n: The prefix وَ means 'and'
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
Adding ة changes it from 'covering' to mean the place that is covered with plants
Adding ات makes it plural (more than one garden)
The ٍ shows this word is connected to عُيُونٍ (springs) that follows it
Added وَ to connect this with what came before it
Added vowel marks to the root letters to make the word for one eye/spring
First step in making the word plural - changing the first vowel
Second step in plural formation - changing the second vowel
Final step in plural - adding و makes it a complete plural noun
Adds tanween kasra to show this word is being described by something else
Adds و with fatha to connect this word to the previous word وَجَنَّاتٍ (gardens)
This is a special word used at the start of sentences to make what follows more emphatic. In this ayah, it emphasizes the speaker's fear mentioned in أَخَافُ (I fear)

steps.m: indeed I (subject of أَخَافُ - I fear)
steps.n: Added ي at the end to show 'I' am speaking. This connects to the verb أَخَافُ (I fear) that follows

steps.m: indeed I (subject of أَخَافُ - I fear)
steps.n: Changed the writing style of ي to ى with an alif mark (ٓ) on top. This is just a spelling variation that doesn't change the meaning
Added vowels to connect letters. The و changes to ا because it's between two vowels.
Added أَ at beginning to show 'I' and ُ at end to show present tense. This word connects to إِنِّىٓ (indeed I) to emphasize the speaker's fear.
This is a preposition that shows something is affecting or coming upon something else. Here it connects to عَذَابَ (punishment) in the ayah.
The ى changes to يْ to prepare for attaching the pronoun
Added كُمْ which means 'you all' when attached to the end of a word
Added vowel marks to connect the letters together and show this is a past-tense verb
Added alif after second letter to change from action to the result of the action
Added fatha at the end because this word is being acted upon by the verb أَخَافُ (I fear) in the phrase 'I fear upon you the punishment'
Root letters put into a simple noun pattern with fatha-sukoon vowels to make the word for 'day'. Owned by the word 'punishment' in this ayah.
Added the kasratan (ٍ) to show this is indefinite (a day) and is possessed by the word عَذَابَ (punishment).
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
The ي between the second and third root letters makes this an adjective showing something has a strong quality
The tanween kasra (ٍ) at the end shows this is describing something general or indefinite, like saying 'a great day' instead of 'the great day'
Adding vowel marks to make basic past tense verb
The middle letter و changed to ا, and وا was added at the end to show many people did the action
Adding fatha marks makes it a past tense verb meaning 'made equal'
Changed from a verb (doing something) to a noun meaning equal/same by adding hamza at the end
Added double damma marks to show this is an indefinite noun in the subject position
This is a basic preposition that shows something is on top of or affecting something else. In this ayah, it's used to show how something affects 'us'.
The ى changes to يْ to prepare for attaching the next part. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.
Added نَآ which means 'us'. In this ayah, this whole word is connected to سَوَآءٌ (equal), showing that something is equal regardless of its effect upon us.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
The تَ at the end shows you (male) did the action
Adding أَ at the beginning turns this into a question (like adding 'did' at the start in English)

steps.m: or (connecting the previous question with another option)
steps.n: This word connects two parts of a question, showing alternatives. In this ayah, it connects 'whether you warned us' with 'or did not warn us', showing the people are saying both options are the same to them.
This is a negative particle that changes the meaning of the verb after it (تَكُن) to show something did not happen in the past. It's like adding 'did not' in English.
Added تَ at start to show 'you' or 'she' is doing the action. This comes after لَمْ (not) in the ayah, which is why it means 'you/she are/is not'
The و is dropped because it comes after لَمْ which requires this shortened form
Basic preposition showing where something comes from or what group it belongs to. Here it connects to الْوٰعِظِينَ (the warners/advisors) to show which group is being discussed.
Added fatha at the end because this word comes before ال. This makes it easier to pronounce.
Added shadda on first letter because it comes after a word ending in a vowel (سَوَآءٌ), making the م sound stronger.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
This pattern shows someone who does the action regularly. The word follows مِن (from/of) in the ayah.
Adding ال makes it specific rather than general
Adding ين makes it plural and shows it follows مِن. The alif becomes long (ٰ) in this form.
In this ayah, إِنْ is used as a negation particle, not a conditional. It works with إِلَّا to mean 'this is nothing but...'
Basic demonstrative pronoun for pointing to something nearby
Added هٰ at the beginning to make it clearer we're pointing to something. The small alif (ٰ) above ه is part of this structure.
Added fatha (َ) and lengthened the final alif (آ) to help with pronunciation and add emphasis when speaking
This is a basic particle that creates exceptions. It has no root because it's a fixed word in Arabic.

steps.m: except for/but/only (excluding خُلُقُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ from what came before)
steps.n: Adding the shadda and alif strengthens the exception meaning and connects it to what follows. In this ayah, it's excluding 'the way of the ancient ones' from something mentioned before.
Adding the vowels ُ (damma) changes it from a verb into a noun meaning someone's nature
The final damma shows this word is the subject in the sentence
Added vowels and doubled the middle letter to show intensity of being first
Added ين to make it plural, referring to people who came before

steps.m: the ancient ones, the predecessors (possessed by خُلُقُ)
steps.n: Added 'the' (ال) at the start to make it specific, and a fatha mark (َ) at the end because it belongs to the word خُلُقُ (meaning ways/behavior)

steps.m: not (negates نَحْنُ بِمُعَذَّبِينَ - we will be punished)
steps.n: This is a special word that negates the statement that follows it. Here it's saying that something will not happen.
The و connects this phrase to what came before it, like saying 'and' in English
This is a basic pronoun meaning 'we' - it doesn't come from a root like most Arabic words do

steps.m: we (as the subject of بِمُعَذَّبِينَ - being punished)
steps.n: The vowel marks show this is the subject (doer) in the sentence. Here it's connected to the word بِمُعَذَّبِينَ (being punished) to say 'we are not being punished'
Added shadda to show intense punishment and connected letters with fatha
Added مُ at start to change from 'punished' to 'person being punished'
Added ين to make it plural, referring to multiple people being punished

steps.m: among those who are severely punished (predicate of نَحْنُ)
steps.n: Added بِ at start to mean 'among'. This word completes the meaning of نَحْنُ (we) in the phrase 'we are not among those being punished'
Connected the root letters with fatha to form basic past tense verb

steps.m: to strongly reject something as false
steps.n: Doubling the middle letter makes the meaning stronger and more intensive, changing from simple 'lying' to 'strongly rejecting as false'

steps.m: and so he strongly rejected as false
steps.n: The prefix فَ means 'and so' or 'then', showing this action happened as a result of something before it

steps.m: and so they strongly rejected as false
steps.n: The و suffix changes the subject from singular to plural (they)

steps.m: and they rejected him as false
steps.n: The ه suffix adds the object 'him' to the verb
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
Adding أ at beginning makes it mean 'cause to perish' instead of just 'perish'
فَ shows this happened as a result of what came before (they denied him, so we destroyed them)
نَا shows 'we' did the action (Allah often uses 'We' to show majesty)
هُمْ shows who was destroyed (them - referring to the people who denied the messenger)
Adding the shadda (doubled ن) changes this from a basic word meaning 'if/that' into a stronger word that adds emphasis to what follows. It's like adding 'definitely' or 'truly' in English.
Basic particle showing location or position inside something
Added ى at the end to complete the word. In this ayah, it shows the location 'in that' referring to the story just mentioned.
This is the basic word used to point to something nearby
The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) to show distance
Adding ل with kasra (لِ) helps show more distance
The كَ at the end makes it clear we're pointing to something specific that was already mentioned

steps.m: to show, indicate, or be a sign

steps.n: Adding ة turns this into a concrete noun meaning 'sign'
Added لَ at the beginning to emphasize that this is definitely a sign. This works with إِنَّ earlier in the verse to show strong emphasis.
Added the double-line mark (ً) at the end to show this word connects to إِنَّ (inna) earlier in the sentence
Added fatha to show correct pronunciation. ما is a versatile word that can mean 'not' or 'what' depending on context. Here it means 'not' because it's negating the verb that follows it (كان).
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it. This و means 'and', connecting this negative statement to the previous verse.
Added vowel marks (fathas) to make it mean 'he was' in the past
Added alif after first letter to make it a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter)
Added vowels to make it a past tense verb
Changed from regular verb to comparative meaning 'more'
Added pronoun هُم to show who is being referred to. This word is the subject of كَانَ (was) in this ayah.
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed from a past action (he trusted) to describe a person who believes/trusts in Allah
Added ين to show multiple believers. This word is affected by كَانَ (was) in the sentence.
The doubled م adds emphasis, showing these are strong or firm in belief
This is an emphasis word that makes what comes next (your Lord) more important. It's like saying 'Listen! This is important!'
The و at the start connects this to the previous statement, like saying 'and' in English. It links this emphasis about your Lord to what was said before.

steps.m: to care for, nurture, develop to completion

steps.n: Added fatha and doubled the middle letter to create a noun meaning 'one who does this action perfectly'
Added كَ to show possession (your). This word is the noun of إِنَّ in the phrase وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ (and indeed, your Lord), which is why it has fatha at the end.
This is a basic pronoun that refers back to 'your Lord' (رَبَّكَ) mentioned just before this word in the ayah

steps.m: surely He (referring to رَبَّكَ - your Lord) is
steps.n: The لَ at the start adds emphasis because this word is part of إِنَّ...لَ construction which shows strong emphasis, like saying 'most certainly' or 'indeed truly'
Added ي in the middle and vowels to show this describes someone with a very strong quality
Added the u-vowel at the end to show this word is the subject
Added 'the' at the start to make it specific/definite

steps.m: to be merciful, to care deeply for others

steps.n: Added ي between ح and م with vowels to show someone who has this quality strongly

steps.m: one who is very merciful (describing اللَّهِ)
steps.n: Added ُ at the end to show this describes Allah in this ayah

steps.m: The One who is very merciful (describing اللَّهِ)
steps.n: Adds the definite article ال to make the noun definite
Adding shadda (ّ) on ر makes it the strongest possible form of mercy, used as one of Allah's names
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb

steps.m: he strongly denied as false (subject of ثَمُودُ)
steps.n: The doubled middle letter (shadda) makes the meaning more intensive and causative

steps.m: she strongly denied as false (referring to ثَمُودُ which is grammatically feminine)
steps.n: The suffix ت shows this is feminine because ثَمُودُ (Thamud) is treated as a feminine word even though it refers to a tribe/people

steps.m: Thamud (name of an ancient Arabian tribe)
steps.n: This is a proper noun (name) that doesn't come from a root. It refers to an ancient Arabian tribe that rejected their prophet Salih.
The ُ (damma) at the end shows that Thamud is doing the action in this sentence - they are the ones who denied the messengers.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'sent' to 'messenger' by adding مُ at start to show the person receiving the sending
Added ين to make it plural, showing there are many messengers
Added ال to specify these particular messengers. This word is what Thamud denied/rejected in the sentence.
This is a time word that shows something happened in the past. In this ayah, it connects to when Salih spoke to his people.
Added fathas to make it past tense. This shows someone (he) did the action in the past.
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb
Added fatha to show the vowel sound
Added هُمْ at the end to show who is being referred to. This word connects to صٰلِحٌ (Salih) in this ayah, as he is speaking to them.
The ُ (damma) at the end shows this is in the nominative case, like when 'brother' is the subject of a sentence

steps.m: their brother (subject of قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Adds possessive pronoun هم (their)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb

steps.m: righteous one, one who is righteous
steps.n: Changes from verb (being righteous) to noun (righteous person)

steps.m: question word (used to start yes/no questions)
steps.n: This is the question particle that turns a statement into a question, like adding a question mark in English

steps.m: Will you not? (used to encourage or prompt)
steps.n: When أ combines with لا (meaning 'not'), it creates a gentle way to encourage someone to do something, like saying 'won't you?' in English

steps.m: to protect or guard against harm

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added تَ at start to show 'you' and changed vowels to show present tense
Added another ت with shadda to show doing action to yourself
Added ون at the end to show multiple people doing the action
This is a special word called a particle that adds emphasis to statements
The ن doubles and gets a kasra (i sound) to connect with the pronoun 'I' that comes after it
Adding ى at the end shows this is about 'me/I'. This word connects to رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ (trustworthy messenger) to mean 'Indeed I am a trustworthy messenger.'
This is a preposition that shows something is being given or directed to someone. Here it connects to رَسُولٌ (messenger) to show who the messenger is for.
The كم at the end shows this is directed to multiple people being addressed. In the ayah, it's showing that the messenger (رَسُولٌ) is for all of them.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Adding و between second and third letters changes it from 'sending' to 'someone who sends/delivers messages a lot'
The double damma (tanween) shows this is talking about a messenger. From context, this refers to the messenger sent to the people of Thamud.
Added basic vowels to connect the root letters
Added ي to show this is a lasting quality rather than just an action
Added tanwin (double damma) to show this is describing the messenger mentioned in the previous word رَسُولٌ
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Adding تّ changes meaning to doing the action to oneself
Added فَ at beginning to mean 'so/then'

steps.m: so protect yourselves! (command to a group)
steps.n: Changed from 'he protected himself' to 'you all protect yourselves!' by adding وا to make it a command for a group
Changed the word to the unique name for The One True God by adding ال and merging letters

steps.m: Allah (as the object of فَٱتَّقُوا۟ - so fear/be mindful of)
steps.n: Added shadda to show emphasis of ل, changed initial alif to wasl form, and added fatha at end to show this is the object of the command 'fear/be mindful of'
Changed from root letters to a present participle form meaning 'one who obeys'
Added hamza with fatha to create command form
Added و to make the command plural - addressing multiple people
Added نِ to show that 'me' is the object being obeyed
Added و with fatha to connect this command with the previous phrase فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ (so fear Allah)
Added fatha to show correct pronunciation
Changed the regular alif (ا) to a long alif (آ) - this is just a writing variation that means the same thing

steps.m: and not (negates أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ - I ask you)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before. This word negates the verb أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ (I ask you) that comes after it.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed to present tense first person by adding أَ at start and ُ at end

steps.m: I ask you all (in وَمَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ - and I do not ask you for any payment for it)
steps.n: Added كُمْ at the end to show who is being asked. This word is part of a larger phrase saying 'I do not ask you for any payment for it'
Added vowel marks to show the correct pronunciation of the basic preposition
Changed the ending to prepare for attaching the pronoun. The ي with sukun (ْ) is added when connecting to pronouns

steps.m: upon him/it (referring back to أَجْر/reward mentioned earlier)
steps.n: Added the pronoun ه (him/it) with kasra (ِ) to show who/what is being referred to. This connects back to the word أَجْر (reward) mentioned earlier in the ayah
Basic preposition that in this context shows what type or amount of reward, connecting to the word أَجْرٍ (reward) that comes after it
Connected the letters and added vowel marks to make it a noun meaning the reward itself
Added tanween kasra to show this is the object of the word مِنْ (from) in the phrase 'from any reward'
Basic negative particle that negates the verb 'I ask you' in this verse. It's different from the conditional 'if' إِنْ - here it means 'not' because it's negating a present tense verb.
Added vowel marks to create the basic noun form. This changes it from an action (paying) to the thing itself (payment/reward).
Added kasra to connect with the coming ي
Added ى with fatha to show possession - meaning this is 'my' reward
This is a basic exception particle that shows something is being excluded from what came before it

steps.m: except (excluding أَجْرِىَ 'my reward' from أَجْرٍ 'a reward')
steps.n: The shadda and alif are added to strengthen and complete the meaning. In this ayah, it's excluding 'my reward' from the general concept of 'a reward' mentioned before.
Basic preposition showing something is on top of or responsible for something else
Added alif maksura (ىٰ) at the end to show this is the end of the word. In this ayah, it shows the prophet is not asking for any reward upon/for delivering the message.

steps.m: to take care of, raise, nurture

steps.n: Added fatha and doubled the middle letter to show complete mastery and lordship
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to what comes after it (العالمين)

steps.m: to know deeply or mark something

steps.n: Forms a noun meaning 'that which is known' - used for 'world' because it's marked out or known by its boundaries
Makes the word plural by adding ين, showing there are many worlds
Adds ال to make it specific - these are all the worlds that exist. The word is possessed by رَبِّ (Lord) in رَبِّ ٱلْعٰلَمِينَ (Lord of the worlds).

steps.m: to leave something behind or abandon it

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed the vowels to show that the action happened to something rather than someone doing it
Added تُ at the beginning to change from past tense to present tense, and changed the ending to ُ to show it's happening now
Added ون to show it's talking about many people
Added أَ at start to make it a question

steps.m: in (location within مَا هٰهُنَآ - what is here)
steps.n: Basic preposition showing location or containment. In this ayah, it connects to 'what is here' to show where something is located.

steps.m: in (location within مَا هٰهُنَآ - what is here)
steps.n: The ي at the end is written as ى when it comes at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
This is a relative pronoun referring to things or possessions mentioned in the context. In this ayah, it's referring to the luxuries and comforts the people were enjoying.
Added هٰ at the beginning to show something is close by. This is like pointing to something near you.
The alif at the end is lengthened to add emphasis, like saying 'right here!'
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb about feeling safe
Changed from past tense 'he felt safe' to present form 'one who feels safe' - like changing 'ran' to 'runner'

steps.m: feeling safe ones (object of تُتْرَكُونَ - will you be left)
steps.n: Added plural ending to show multiple people. This word is affected by the verb تُتْرَكُونَ (will you be left) in the ayah.
This is a basic preposition that shows location or being inside something
Added kasra (ِ) under ف to show it's connecting to the word after it
Changed the ي to ى which is just a writing style change - it means the same thing
Added fatha on first letter and doubled the middle letter to show complete covering

steps.m: a garden (because it's covered with trees)
steps.n: The doubled ن gives intensified meaning of covering, feminine ة makes it a noun for garden
Adding ات makes it plural for more than two gardens. This word comes after فِى (in/within) in the ayah.
The ٍ shows this word is affected by the word فِى before it
Added vowels to the root letters to make the word mean 'a spring of water'
Changed vowels to make it plural, showing there are many springs
Added وَ to connect this word to gardens (جَنَّاتٍ) mentioned before it

steps.m: and springs (following جَنَّاتٍ in the genitive case)
steps.n: Added tanween kasra to match the case of جَنَّاتٍ since both are objects of فِي (in)
Changed from root meaning 'to plant' into the noun meaning 'crop' by adding vowels
Changed vowels and added و to make plural form
Added conjunction to connect with previous phrase
Added tanween kasra to show this is part of a description of things the people had
Added tanwin kasra to show this is indefinite and is being described by what comes after it
Added و to connect this with the previous word وَزُرُوعٍ (and crops/plantations)
Changed the root into a noun meaning the part of a palm tree that emerges containing the dates
Added the ُ to show this is the subject being described as هَضِيمٌ (fine/soft) in this ayah
Added ها which refers back to the palm trees (نَخْلٍ) mentioned just before this word
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changed from a verb (to arrange) into a describing word meaning 'well-arranged/compact', telling us how the date palm's fruit stalks look
Adding tanween dammah makes the word indefinite
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Changed from past to present tense by adding ت prefix
Added ون to show multiple people are doing the action

steps.m: and you all carve (from مِنَ ٱلْجِبَالِ - from the mountains)
steps.n: Added و with fatha at start to mean 'and', connecting this action to what came before. This word connects to 'from the mountains' in the ayah.
Basic preposition showing something comes from or out of something else
Added fatha at the end because it connects to the next word الْجِبَالِ (the mountains). This helps make the pronunciation flow better between the words.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic noun
Changed vowel pattern to show many mountains
Added ال to make it specific mountains
Added kasra at the end because it comes after the word مِنَ (from)
Changes from root meaning into a concrete noun meaning 'house'
Changes singular 'house' into plural form by adding damma marks and و

steps.m: houses (as objects being carved out from mountains)
steps.n: Added -an ending to show these are the houses being carved out (object of the verb 'carve')
Added alif after first letter to show who has this quality. This describes the people who are doing the carving (تَنْحِتُونَ) of houses in the mountains.
Added ين to show this describes many people. These are the people who are skillfully carving houses in the mountains.
Added fatha at the end to show this word is in the accusative case, meaning it's the object of something
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Adding تّ changes meaning to doing the action to oneself
Added فَ at beginning to mean 'so/then'

steps.m: so protect yourselves! (command to a group)
steps.n: Changed from 'he protected himself' to 'you all protect yourselves!' by adding وا to make it a command for a group
Changed from general word for deity to specific name for Allah by adding ال and merging letters

steps.m: Allah (as the object of فَٱتَّقُوا۟ - so fear/be mindful of)
steps.n: Added shadda on ل to show emphasis, fatha at end to show this is the object being acted upon, and changed initial alif to wasl form since it comes after another word
Changed root letters into past tense verb by adding the vowel 'aa' in middle and 'a' at end
Adding ُ at end shows this is a normal present tense verb
Changes from 'I obey' to 'all of you obey!' (command)
Adds object pronoun 'me' to show who should be obeyed
Adds conjunction to connect with previous command فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ (so fear Allah)
This is the basic negative particle that means 'not' or 'do not' when used in commands
Added و at the beginning to connect this negative command with what came before. This word connects to تُطِيعُوٓا۟ (obey) which comes after it.
Root letters connected and vowels added to make past tense 'he obeyed'. Middle letter و became ا which is common in hollow verbs.
Changed from past to present tense by changing vowels. The long ا became ي because it comes after a kasra (i) sound
Added تُ at beginning to show 'you' are doing the action

steps.m: you all obey (أَمْرَ ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ - the command of the wasteful ones)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show multiple people are doing the action. The alif after و is just for writing style.
Changed from verb 'to command' into the noun 'command' - this is what people are being told not to obey
Added fatha at the end to show this is what's being acted upon in the phrase 'do not obey the command'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past-tense verb
Describes someone who does this action as a habit or characteristic

steps.m: those who go beyond limits (object of أَمْرَ)
steps.n: Makes the word plural. This word is the object of أَمْرَ (command) in the phrase 'command of the transgressors'

steps.m: the ones who go beyond limits (object of أَمْرَ)
steps.n: Makes the word specific by adding 'the'

steps.m: those who (refers to a group of people)
steps.n: This is a relative pronoun used to describe or specify a group. The ال makes it definite, and ين/ن makes it plural masculine.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense form
Changes from past tense 'damaged' to present tense 'damages' by adding يُ at the start
The ون ending shows this is about the group mentioned by الَّذِينَ (those who)
This is a basic preposition showing location. In this ayah, it connects the corruption (يُفْسِدُونَ) to where it happens (ٱلْأَرْضِ - the earth).
The ي at the end is written as ى when it's at the end of a word. This is just a writing change - it doesn't change the meaning.
Added vowels to make it a basic noun meaning earth or land
Added ال to make it specific and ِ to show it comes after the word فِي (in/on)
This is a negative particle that negates the verb يُصْلِحُونَ (they reform/make right) that comes after it
Added و at the beginning to connect this negative statement to the previous description, showing these are people who both corrupt the earth AND do not reform it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
The يُ prefix shows this is happening now or in the future. The vowel pattern change makes it mean 'to cause something to become fixed/right' rather than just 'to be right'
The ون ending shows this is about a group doing the action. This word describes 'those who' (الَّذِينَ) from earlier in the verse.
Connected the letters and changed و to ا because it's between two fathas. This makes it past tense.
Added وا at the end to show many people did this action. This word is connected to إِنَّمَآ (indeed) which comes after it.
Added optional marks (elongation sign ٓ and small high rounded zero ۟) that don't change the meaning
This is an emphasis particle that makes statements stronger, like adding 'indeed' or 'certainly' in English
Adding ما changes the meaning from emphasis to restriction, showing that something is 'only' or 'just' true. Here it restricts the following statement about being bewitched.
The alif is lengthened at the end to help with pronunciation when stopping at this word. This doesn't change the meaning.
This is the basic form of the second person pronoun

steps.m: you (masculine singular, subject of مِنَ ٱلْمُسَحَّرِينَ)
steps.n: Added ت to specify this is for addressing one male person. This pronoun is the subject of the phrase 'from those bewitched'

This is a preposition that shows something belongs to a group or comes from somewhere. Here it connects to the word ٱلْمُسَحَّرِينَ (the bewitched ones) to show Salih is being called one of the bewitched ones.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb

steps.m: one who is affected by magic
steps.n: Added مُ at start to show who receives the action, and doubled the middle letter to show intensity

steps.m: those who are affected by magic (after مِنَ)
steps.n: Added ين to show it's plural and comes after the word مِنَ (from/of)
Added ال to make it specific and َ at the end because it comes after مِنَ
This is an invariable particle that does not derive from a root. The alif is elongated to مَآ instead of مَا for emphasis.
Added fatha to show correct pronunciation

steps.m: not (negating أَنتَ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ - you are only a human)
steps.n: The alif is elongated to add emphasis to the negation. This word is negating the statement that follows it in the ayah.
This is the basic form of the pronoun meaning 'you'

steps.m: you (when speaking to one male person)
steps.n: These are actually two different words - أَنْ means 'that' while أَنتَ is the separate word for 'you'. They are not related through transformation.
This is a special word used to make exceptions. In this ayah, it connects 'you are' (أَنتَ) with 'a human' (بَشَرٌ) to say 'you are nothing except a human'
Added fathas to connect the letters and form the basic noun. This pattern creates a noun for a being or thing characterized by the root meaning.
The tanwin (double damma) shows this is indefinite (any human being). This word describes what 'you' (أَنتَ) are in the sentence.

steps.m: to be like or similar to

steps.n: Added vowel marks to create a noun meaning 'similar to' or 'like'
Added damma at the end to show this word is in the nominative case
Added نا at the end to show possession - 'our' or 'us'
Added shadda on م to emphasize the sound
Changed to command form by adding hamza with sukun and kasra at end
Added فَ at beginning to show this command follows from what was said before

steps.m: to show, make clear, or be evident

steps.n: Changed the root into a noun that means one clear sign or miracle. The first two letters combine into a long آ.
Added tanwin kasra to show this is indefinite (like 'a' in English) and is being acted upon by the preposition that comes next

steps.m: with a sign (object of فَأْتِ - so bring forth)
steps.n: Added the preposition بِ meaning 'with'. This word is what should be brought forth according to the command فَأْتِ (so bring forth) in the ayah.

steps.m: if (introduces condition for كُنتَ - you were)
steps.n: This is a conditional particle that introduces a condition. In this ayah, it connects 'if you were' (كُنتَ) with what comes before it, meaning 'if you were among the truthful ones, [then prove it]'.
Added basic vowels to connect the letters

steps.m: you were (addressing the disbelievers who doubt the Prophet ﷺ)
steps.n: The root word 'to be' changes to 'you were' by dropping و and adding تَ at the end to show it's talking about 'you' in the past
This is the basic form of the word that shows something comes from somewhere or belongs to a group
Added fatha at the end to connect smoothly to the next word الصَّادِقِينَ (the truthful ones). This shows we're talking about being 'from among the truthful ones'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed from a past tense verb (he was truthful) to a describing word (truthful person) - like changing 'ran' to 'runner'
Makes it plural to describe many truthful people

1. Added ال (the) at the start to make it specific
2. The word comes after مِنَ (from/of) in the ayah, which is why it has the ending َ
Added vowel marks to make it past tense. This shows someone did the action in the past.
The و (weak letter) changes to ا, which is a common change in Arabic verbs
Basic demonstrative pronoun meaning 'this'
Added ذا to specify something near the speaker
Changed ending to هِ with kasra to make it feminine
Added small ي (ۦ) as a pronunciation helper. This word is pointing to the she-camel (نَاقَةٌ) mentioned right after it.
The alif makes it a noun pattern for animals, and the ta marbuta shows it's feminine

steps.m: a she-camel (subject of هٰذِهِۦ - this is)
steps.n: Added tanween dammah to show this is the subject of the sentence and is indefinite (a she-camel rather than the she-camel). This she-camel is being pointed to with هٰذِهِۦ (this is) in the ayah.
Starting with the basic preposition ل meaning 'for' or 'to'
Adding emphasis with shadda to show importance (this is about the she-camel's drinking rights)

steps.m: surely for her (referring to the she-camel نَاقَةٌ)
steps.n: Adding ها to show this is about the she-camel mentioned earlier in the ayah

steps.m: to drink or take a share of water

steps.n: Changes the verb into a noun meaning 'a portion for drinking' or 'drinking share'. In this ayah, it's talking about taking turns with a camel for drinking water on specific days.
The double-damma ending shows this is the subject of the sentence, meaning 'a drinking portion' belongs to someone.
Added كُمْ to show this is directed to multiple people being spoken to

steps.m: and for you all (connected to شِرْبٌ - drinking)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase to the previous statement about the she-camel's drinking. This shows both the she-camel and the people have a share in drinking.

steps.m: to drink or take a portion of liquid
a drinking time/turn or share of water
steps.n: Changes the verb into a noun meaning 'a specific drinking time' or 'allocated share of water'

steps.m: a drinking time/turn or share of water (subject of لَّهَا - belongs to her)
steps.n: The ُ shows this is the subject in the sentence. Here it's specifically talking about the she-camel's allocated turn/share of drinking water.
Root letters y-w-m get vowels (fatha-sukoon) to make the basic noun meaning 'day'. This word is connected to شِرْبُ (drinking) to show 'drinking of a day'.
Added tanween kasra at the end to show this is indefinite (a day) and is possessed by شِرْبُ (drinking/share of water).
Basic vowels (fatha) added to make past tense 'he knew'
Changes from verb 'he knew' to noun meaning 'something that is known'
Doubling the م adds emphasis to show something is definitely/surely known
Adds tanween kasra to show this word describes 'day' (يَوْمٍ) in the phrase 'a known day'
Added fatha to show this is the basic negative particle that stops something from happening

steps.m: and not (connects to previous statement and negates تَمَسُّوهَا)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this negative command with what came before it
Connected the root letters and added shadda to show emphasis on the middle letter
Adds تَ at start to make it 'you' in present tense
Adds plural masculine subject pronoun suffix
Adds ها at the end to show a feminine thing being touched (like 'her' or 'it')
Changed the verb into a noun by adding damma on first letter
Adds preposition ب meaning 'with/by'
Adds tanween kasra (ٍ) to show this is indefinite ('any harm') and is affected by the preposition بِ. This word completes the meaning of 'touch/harm' (تَمَسُّوهَا) in the verse.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, sukun on hamza, and damma at end
Added فَ to show this happens as a result of something before it

steps.m: and then he will seize you all (as a warning in this ayah)
steps.n: Added كُمْ (kum) at the end to show 'you all' are the ones being seized - changes from general 'he seizes' to specifically 'he seizes you'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Adding alif after second root letter changes the verb into a noun meaning the result of the action
Adding damma at the end shows this word is doing the action in the sentence - the punishment is what seizes them
Connected the letters with vowel marks to form the basic noun. The و becomes silent with sukun.

steps.m: of a day (possessed by عَذَابُ - punishment)
steps.n: Added tanween kasra to show this word is possessed by عَذَابُ (punishment). When two nouns are connected like this in Arabic, the second one gets this ending to show it belongs to the first one.
Added vowels (fatha and damma) to make it mean 'became great' in the past
Changed from a verb (became great) to an adjective (great/mighty) by adding ي. This describes يَوْمٍ (day) in the ayah.
Added tanween kasra to show this word follows يَوْمٍ in the genitive case
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb. This shows one person did the action in the past.
Added وا to show many people did this action together
Added ها to show what they slaughtered - the she-camel mentioned earlier

steps.m: and so they slaughtered her (the she-camel)
steps.n: Added فَ at the start to show this happened as a result of something before it

steps.m: to enter/become in a state or condition

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Form IV verb pattern that shows entering into a state or condition
Adding وا shows a group of people did this action
Adding فَ connects this to the previous action, showing it happened as a result. The word نٰدِمِينَ after it shows what state they entered into - they became regretful.
This pattern is used for verbs that express feelings or emotions
Changed the verb into a participle showing who does the action
Made the word plural and put it in accusative case because it's the predicate of أَصْبَحُوا (they became). The alif changed to a long alif (ٰ) in this form.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create past tense verb. This word is connected to العَذَابُ (the punishment) which comes right after it in the ayah.
Adding فَ shows this action happened as a consequence of what came before

steps.m: and then he took/seized them (referring to the people of Thamud)
steps.n: The هُمُ suffix shows who received the action - in this case referring back to the people of Thamud who were mentioned earlier. The final ُ is added because this word connects to العَذَابُ which comes next.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Long ا after second root letter makes this a noun meaning the result of punishment. This word is the subject (doer) of the verb أخذ (seized) in فَأَخَذَهُمُ (then it seized them).
Added ال to make it specific/definite
Added damma to indicate nominative case

steps.m: indeed (emphasizes فِى ذٰلِكَ لَءَايَةً - 'in that is a sign')
steps.n: The shadda (ّ) doubles the ن letter to make the emphasis stronger. This word introduces and emphasizes an important statement that follows it.
Basic preposition showing location or position
Added ى at the end to complete the preposition. This preposition shows location within the thing mentioned after it (ذٰلِكَ/that).
Basic demonstrative pronoun for pointing to something nearby
The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) to show distance
Added ل with kasra to strengthen the meaning of distance
Added ك to make it more specific and emphatic
The root letters combine with the first two letters merging into a long آ sound
The ة (taa marboota) marks this as a feminine singular noun
The ً (tanween fatha) shows this is the object of إِنَّ (indeed/certainly) in this ayah
The لَ adds emphasis, working together with إِنَّ to strongly emphasize that this is definitely a sign
Added fatha to show correct pronunciation
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before. This word modifies كَانَ (was) in the phrase.
Adding the fatha (َ) shows this is a past tense verb
Adding the second fatha (َ) completes the past tense verb pattern
Added أ at the beginning and vowel marks to show comparison, like changing 'many' to 'more' or 'most' in English
Added ُ at the end to show this is the subject (doer) of the verb كَانَ (was) in this ayah
Added هُم at the end to mean 'them', showing who the 'most' refers to
Added مُ at the start to show who does the action, like changing 'believe' to 'believer' in English
Added ين to show we're talking about many believers. This word is being affected by the verb كَانَ (was) in this ayah.
The doubled م makes the meaning stronger - these are very committed believers
This is an emphasis particle that makes listeners pay attention to what comes next. In this ayah, it emphasizes the statement about 'your Lord'
The و at the start connects this statement to what came before it, showing this is part of a continuing message
Added fatha marks to connect the letters together

steps.m: Lord (one who completely cares for others)
steps.n: The doubled middle letter (shadda) makes this an intensive form, emphasizing the complete nature of the nurturing
Added كَ to show it belongs to you. This word is affected by إِنَّ which comes before it in the ayah.
This is a basic pronoun that refers back to 'your Lord' mentioned earlier in the ayah

steps.m: surely He is (referring to رَبَّكَ - your Lord)
steps.n: The لَ at the start adds emphasis because this is making a strong statement about Allah's attributes (ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ - the Mighty, the Merciful) that follow
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
This pattern shows someone or something that has a very strong quality
Adding ال makes it refer to a specific mighty one
The ُ shows this word is the subject of the sentence
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Changes from 'he was merciful' (verb) to 'very merciful' (describing word) by following a special pattern that makes it stronger
Adds two damma marks at the end to show this is describing something in a general way
Adds 'the' at start to make it specific/definite. The ر gets doubled (shadda) after ال

steps.m: The Intensely Merciful (attribute of رَبَّكَ - your Lord)

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make it a past tense verb

steps.m: he strongly denied as false (subject of قَوْمُ)
steps.n: The doubled middle letter (shadda) makes the meaning more intense. This word is part of قَوْمُ (people) who did the denying.

steps.m: she strongly denied as false (refers to قَوْمُ which is feminine singular here)
steps.n: The suffix ت shows this word is feminine singular, referring to قَوْمُ (people) which is treated as feminine singular in Arabic even though it means a group
Changes verb root into a noun meaning 'a group that stands together'
The ُ ending shows this word is doing the action in the sentence
This is a proper name, so we start with the word itself rather than a root
The tanween kasra (ٍ) here shows this word is possessed by/connected to something. From context, it means 'the people of Lot' since قَوْمُ (people) comes right before it.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Added مُ at start to show someone receiving the action (like 'sent one' or 'messenger')
Added ين to make it plural, referring to multiple messengers
Added ال to specify these particular messengers. This word is the object (receiver) of the verb كَذَّبَتْ (denied/rejected) in this ayah.
This is a time particle that introduces a past event. Here it begins the story of Prophet Lut speaking to his people.
This is the three-letter root that means speaking or saying
Added short vowels (fatha) to make it past tense 'he said'
Changed قَوَلَ to قَالَ by replacing weak letter و with ا
Added fatha to show the basic vowel of the preposition
Added هُمْ which shows this is happening to or for multiple people. In this ayah, it refers to the people being addressed by Lut.
This root relates to brotherhood and fraternal relationships
Changed the root letters into the basic word for 'brother' by adding a fatha mark on the first letter
Added damma and restored the weak letter و to show this is the subject form
Added هُمْ to show this is their brother. This word is the subject (doer) of the verb قَالَ (he said) in this ayah.
This is the basic form of the prophet's name Lot (لوط). In Arabic, proper names often don't come from roots.

steps.m: Lot (as the subject who is speaking)
steps.n: Added double-damma (ٌ) to show this is the subject doing the speaking in this ayah (when he said to them...)
This is the question particle that turns a statement into a question

steps.m: Will you not? (used to encourage تَتَّقُونَ)
steps.n: Adding لا (not) to the question particle creates a rhetorical question that encourages the action. Here it's encouraging the action of تَتَّقُونَ (being mindful of Allah/having taqwa).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Added تَتَّ at beginning to make it mean 'you', and changed the word pattern to show doing the action of protecting yourself (present tense)
Added ون ending to show many people are being addressed
This is a special word that adds emphasis to what comes after it, like when you really want someone to believe what you're saying

steps.m: indeed I (speaking to لَكُمْ - to you all)
steps.n: Added ى at the end to show 'I' am speaking. This connects to the next word لَكُمْ which means 'to you all', showing who I'm speaking to.
This is a preposition that shows something is being directed to someone. In this ayah, it's connecting to the word رَسُولٌ (messenger).

steps.m: to/for you all (referring to the people being addressed)
steps.n: The كُمْ shows this is speaking to multiple people. Combined with the previous لَ, it means something is being directed to multiple people.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changes from a verb (to send) to a noun meaning 'someone who delivers messages a lot' by adding a 'u' sound in the middle
Adds tanween dammah to show this is indefinite (like saying 'a messenger' instead of 'the messenger'). In this ayah, this refers to the messenger sent to the people of Lut.
Added fatha on first letter and added ي with kasra between first and second root letters to make it into an adjective describing someone trustworthy
Added tanwin dammah (ٌ) to show this is describing the messenger (رَسُولٌ) mentioned in the previous word
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added اِتَّ at the beginning to make it mean 'protect oneself' instead of 'protect others'
Added فَ (so/then) at beginning with connecting alif

steps.m: so protect yourselves! (command to a group)
steps.n: Changed to command form for multiple people by adding وا at the end and changing vowels
Changed the word to the unique name for The One True God by adding ال and merging letters

steps.m: Allah (as the object of فَٱتَّقُوا۟ - so fear/be mindful of)
steps.n: Added shadda to show emphasis of ل, changed initial alif to wasl form, and added fatha at end to show this is the object of the verb 'fear/be mindful of'
Changed root letters into past tense verb by adding the 'aa' sound in middle
Adding أ at start changes meaning to 'make someone do the action'
Changes from past tense 'he obeyed' to a command meaning 'all of you obey!'
Adds object pronoun 'me'
Adds conjunction 'and' to connect with previous command (فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ)
This is a basic negative particle that can also be used to ask questions
Added fatha to help with pronunciation
Extended the alif to show emphasis in pronunciation. This is just a writing variation and doesn't change the meaning.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what comes after it. Here it connects to the verb أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ (I ask you).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to present tense 'I' form by adding أَ at start and changing vowels
Added كُمْ at the end to show who is being asked. In this ayah, the prophet is saying he doesn't ask his people for any reward.
When we want to attach something to على, the ى changes to يْ to make attachment easier
Added هِ at the end to show who or what is being referred to. This هِ means 'him' or 'it' depending on context.
This is a basic preposition that shows where something comes from or what it's made of. In this ayah, it connects 'I ask you' with 'reward', showing what is being asked for.
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to make it a noun. This word is the object of the preposition مِنْ (from) in this ayah.
Added tanween kasra (ٍ) at the end to show this is any general reward, not a specific one. This matches how the prophet is saying he's not asking for any reward at all.
This is a negative particle that negates the verb أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ (I ask you) in this context. While إِنْ can mean 'if' in other contexts, here it's being used for negation.
Changed from action to the thing itself (noun form)
Added kasra to show this word is ready to be possessed by someone

steps.m: my reward/payment (object of أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ - I ask you)
steps.n: Added ى with fatha to show this is my reward. This word is what's being asked about in the phrase 'I do not ask you for my reward'
